1: <header>Life cycle assessment and nutrient balance for five different treatment methods for poultry litter </header>

S. Nusselder a, , L.G. de Graaff a , I.Y.R. Odegard C. Vandecasteele b, H.J. Croezen a, 1 a CE Delft, Oude Delft 180, 2611, HH Delft, the Netherlands b Department of Chemical Engineering, KU Leuven, Celestijnenlaan 200F, Box 2424, 3001, Leuven, Belgium
2: Article history: Received 31 July 2019 Received in revised form 10 April 2020 Accepted 21 April 2020 Available online 30 April 2020

<list>Atmospheric ammonia emissions from raw litter cause acidification. 
Greenhouse gases, mainly methane and nitrous oxide, are not only emitted during the raising of livestock, but also during litter handling. In line with the goals set in the Paris Agreement (United Nations, 2015), these emissions will have to be reduced to a minimum by 2050.</list>
3: In several European countries with a large livestock sector, e.g. Belgium, Cyprus, Luxemburg, Malta and the Netherlands, manure production by livestock exceeds the nutrient requirement of the crops grown. In 2015 each of these countries had a gross nitrogen balance of more than 100 kg nitrogen per hectare (Eurostat, 2018). Traditionally manure was part of a natural cycle, in which livestock consumes e.g. grass and releases manure and carbon-containing gases (CO2, CH4), and afterwards manure and carbon in the gases are taken up again by the grass. Intensive livestock farming dras- tically changed this situation, as it is often regionally concentrated, so that manure supply exceeds nutrient requirements of crops grown in the region.
Manure surplus causes several environmental issues (De Vries et al., 2012):
Due to over-fertilization, direct application to land leads to runoff of nitrate to surface water and eventually into the sea, causing eutrophication.

Treatment methods of manure aim at reducing its environ- mental impact and increasing its economic value. The traditional method to utilize manure is direct application to agricultural land, either by broadcast spreading, band spreading, or injection (Chen et al., 2001; Velthof et al., 2010). Alternative technologies for the treatment of manure are (Szogi et al., 2015):

<list>Compaction: pelletizing or baling of solid manure to ease transportation. 
Composting: stabilizing the organic matter. Compost can either be applied directly to land or can be further compacted. 
Anaerobic digestion: variants include digestion of one single type of manure, co-digestion of different types of manure or co- digestion of manure with plant-based material. Anaerobic digestion produces biogas and digestate, which can be applied to land (Esteves et al., 2019) 
Solid-liquid separation: separation of solid and liquid manure to make each type of manure easier to handle. 
Thermal conversion: either combustion, pyrolysis, or gasification. 
Addition of chemical amendments, nitrification-denitrification, etc. to reduce ammonia emissions from manure. 
Extraction of phosphorus: because phosphorus is a valuable resource, several technologies exist to recover phosphorus from manure, including chemical precipitation and wet extraction.</list>
<list>1 Present address: Royal Haskoning DHV, Laan 1914 35, 3818 EX Amersfoort, The Netherlands, harry.croezen@rhdhv.com</list>

Several authors applied Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to assess and compare the environmental benefits of different treatment methods for a range of manure and litter processing systems. Cherubini et al. (2015) did this for four swine manure management systems; Billen et al. (2015) for combustion with electricity pro- duction and for direct land application of poultry litter; Corbala-
Robles et al. (2018) compared biological pig manure treatment with direct land application, Esteves et al. (2019) reviewed LCA of biogas production from manure.

Poultry litter is somewhat special in this context, since (i) it has a high nitrogen content leading to an unattractive N/P balance for direct land application in comparison to other types of litter and (ii) it is relatively dry in comparison to other types of litter, and consequently easier to transport and combust to produce energy. Depending on the market situation and availability of other types of manure such as pig manure, poultry litter is a likely candidate for treatment instead of direct land application.

Kelleher et al. (2002) reviewed technologies for treatment of poultry litter, and mainly discussed composting, anaerobic diges- tion, and direct combustion. Only Billen et al. (2015) and Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 studied the environmental impact of poultry litter treatment. Williams et al. (2016), treat the environmental benefits of using turkey litter as a fuel instead of a fertilizer.

Billen et al. (2015) compare the combustion of poultry litter in a fluidized bed combustor (FBC) to generate heat used to produce electricity, with direct land application of poultry litter. Besides electricity, also ash is obtained that can be used as a fertilizer. The study pointed out that combustion of poultry litter, with subse- quent ash recycling, has two major advantages over its direct land application: (i) the energy content of the biomass is valorized as carbon neutral and renewable energy, and (ii) poultry litter ash, in which valuable components, e.g. phosphate and potassium, are retained, is dry, sterile, odorless and easy to handle and transport. Combustion of poultry litter in an FBC is a unique process: it starts from a by-product, and produces renewable energy, zero waste, and zero wastewater. Billen et al. (2015) take into account that during poultry litter combustion nitrogen is lost as nitrogen gas, as opposed to during direct application to land, and acknowledge the need to, in some cases, apply artificial nitrogen fertilizer to sup- plement the nitrogen need, when poultry litter ash is applied as fertilizer. Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 analyze the characteristics of poultry litter as fuel, describe the different thermal conversion technologies in which it can be utilized, propose measures to in- crease the sustainability of the treatment of poultry litter, and stress the importance of proper use of the ash after thermal conversion and of controlling harmful process emissions. Both studies are limited in scope, as Billen et al. (2015) consider only direct appli- cation of poultry litter and thermal conversion, and Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 only thermal conversion technologies. Neither of these studies takes into account that organic matter contained in the poultry litter increases the soil carbon content (Rice, 2005), and neither study acknowledges that, in addition to nitrogen, in some instances phosphorus and potassium fertilizers must be supplemented.

The present paper focusses on the environmental impact of treatment of poultry litter, and compares in detail five different treatment methods as they are currently applied to Dutch poultry manure. In the Netherlands, poultry raising has a high economic interest with approximately 1.5 billion euro revenue per year (Wageningen University and Research, 2018). In 2018, 97 million hens were grown (CBS, 2019a), leading to 1.4 billion kg of poultry litter (CBS, 2019b). Approximately 10% of the poultry litter is directly applied to land within the Netherlands, 10% is used to produce mushroom compost, 10% for the production of granulates, 35% is combusted by BMC to generate electricity (van Horne et al., 2016). The remainder of the poultry litter is being exported, either as compost or as raw litter. This litter is used for direct application to land, as well as for co-digestion with maize fodder in Germany and as compost in France (van Horne et al., 2016). The division over these different applications of exported poultry litter is unclear.
The results of the study are not only relevant for the Netherlands

but also for other countries and regions with a surplus of poultry litter. During the analysis, care is taken to separate parameters specific to the Dutch case from those that apply in other regions. The objective of this study is as follows: in order to support decision-making in the selection of an adequate treatment of poultry litter, compare the environmental impact of the following five treatment methods:

<list>Direct land application 
Composting 
Composting and granulation (referred to in this paper as gran- ulation, to avoid confusion with the composting treatment method without granulation) 
Co-digestion with fodder maize 
Thermal conversion.</list>
The treatment of poultry litter to produce mushroom compost is not studied because of a lack of publicly available data on this treatment method.

Combustion of poultry litter in an FBC is a unique process: it starts from a by-product, and produces renewable energy, zero waste, and zero wastewater. Billen et al. (2015) take into account that during poultry litter combustion nitrogen is lost as nitrogen gas, as opposed to during direct application to land, and acknowledge the need to, in some cases, apply artificial nitrogen fertilizer to sup- plement the nitrogen need, when poultry litter ash is applied as fertilizer. Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 analyze the characteristics of poultry litter as fuel, describe the different thermal conversion technologies in which it can be utilized, propose measures to in- crease the sustainability of the treatment of poultry litter, and stress the importance of proper use of the ash after thermal conversion and of controlling harmful process emissions. Both studies are limited in scope, as Billen et al. (2015) consider only direct appli- cation of poultry litter and thermal conversion, and Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 only thermal conversion technologies. Neither of these studies takes into account that organic matter contained in the poultry litter increases the soil carbon content (Rice, 2005), and neither study acknowledges that, in addition to nitrogen, in some instances phosphorus and potassium fertilizers must be supplemented.

The present paper focusses on the environmental impact of treatment of poultry litter, and compares in detail five different treatment methods as they are currently applied to Dutch poultry manure. In the Netherlands, poultry raising has a high economic interest with approximately 1.5 billion euro revenue per year (Wageningen University and Research, 2018). In 2018, 97 million hens were grown (CBS, 2019a), leading to 1.4 billion kg of poultry litter (CBS, 2019b). Approximately 10% of the poultry litter is directly applied to land within the Netherlands, 10% is used to produce mushroom compost, 10% for the production of granulates, 35% is combusted by BMC to generate electricity (van Horne et al., 2016). The remainder of the poultry litter is being exported, either as compost or as raw litter. This litter is used for direct application to land, as well as for co-digestion with maize fodder in Germany and as compost in France (van Horne et al., 2016). The division over these different applications of exported poultry litter is unclear.
The results of the study are not only relevant for the Netherlands

but also for other countries and regions with a surplus of poultry litter. During the analysis, care is taken to separate parameters specific to the Dutch case from those that apply in other regions. The objective of this study is as follows: in order to support decision-making in the selection of an adequate treatment of poultry litter, compare the environmental impact of the following five treatment methods:

<list>Direct land application 
Composting 
Composting and granulation (referred to in this paper as gran- ulation, to avoid confusion with the composting treatment method without granulation) 
Co-digestion with fodder maize 
Thermal conversion.</list>
The treatment of poultry litter to produce mushroom compost is not studied because of a lack of publicly available data on this treatment method.
8: In several European countries with a large livestock sector, e.g. Belgium, Cyprus, Luxemburg, Malta and the Netherlands, manure production by livestock exceeds the nutrient requirement of the crops grown. In 2015 each of these countries had a gross nitrogen balance of more than 100 kg nitrogen per hectare (Eurostat, 2018). Traditionally manure was part of a natural cycle, in which livestock consumes e.g. grass and releases manure and carbon-containing gases (CO2, CH4), and afterwards manure and carbon in the gases are taken up again by the grass. Intensive livestock farming dras- tically changed this situation, as it is often regionally concentrated, so that manure supply exceeds nutrient requirements of crops grown in the region.
Manure surplus causes several environmental issues (De Vries et al., 2012):
Due to over-fertilization, direct application to land leads to runoff of nitrate to surface water and eventually into the sea, causing eutrophication.

Treatment methods of manure aim at reducing its environ- mental impact and increasing its economic value. The traditional method to utilize manure is direct application to agricultural land, either by broadcast spreading, band spreading, or injection (Chen et al., 2001; Velthof et al., 2010). Alternative technologies for the treatment of manure are (Szogi et al., 2015):

<list>Compaction: pelletizing or baling of solid manure to ease transportation. 
Composting: stabilizing the organic matter. Compost can either be applied directly to land or can be further compacted. 
Anaerobic digestion: variants include digestion of one single type of manure, co-digestion of different types of manure or co- digestion of manure with plant-based material. Anaerobic digestion produces biogas and digestate, which can be applied to land (Esteves et al., 2019) 
Solid-liquid separation: separation of solid and liquid manure to make each type of manure easier to handle. 
Thermal conversion: either combustion, pyrolysis, or gasification. 
Addition of chemical amendments, nitrification-denitrification, etc. to reduce ammonia emissions from manure. 
Extraction of phosphorus: because phosphorus is a valuable resource, several technologies exist to recover phosphorus from manure, including chemical precipitation and wet extraction.</list>

Several authors applied Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to assess and compare the environmental benefits of different treatment methods for a range of manure and litter processing systems. Cherubini et al. (2015) did this for four swine manure management systems; Billen et al. (2015) for combustion with electricity pro- duction and for direct land application of poultry litter; Corbala-
Robles et al. (2018) compared biological pig manure treatment with direct land application, Esteves et al. (2019) reviewed LCA of biogas production from manure.

Poultry litter is somewhat special in this context, since (i) it has a high nitrogen content leading to an unattractive N/P balance for direct land application in comparison to other types of litter and (ii) it is relatively dry in comparison to other types of litter, and consequently easier to transport and combust to produce energy. Depending on the market situation and availability of other types of manure such as pig manure, poultry litter is a likely candidate for treatment instead of direct land application.

Kelleher et al. (2002) reviewed technologies for treatment of poultry litter, and mainly discussed composting, anaerobic diges- tion, and direct combustion. Only Billen et al. (2015) and Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 studied the environmental impact of poultry litter treatment. Williams et al. (2016), treat the environmental benefits of using turkey litter as a fuel instead of a fertilizer.

Billen et al. (2015) compare the combustion of poultry litter in a fluidized bed combustor (FBC) to generate heat used to produce electricity, with direct land application of poultry litter. Besides electricity, also ash is obtained that can be used as a fertilizer. The study pointed out that combustion of poultry litter, with subse- quent ash recycling, has two major advantages over its direct land application: (i) the energy content of the biomass is valorized as carbon neutral and renewable energy, and (ii) poultry litter ash, in which valuable components, e.g. phosphate and potassium, are retained, is dry, sterile, odorless and easy to handle and transport. Combustion of poultry litter in an FBC is a unique process: it starts from a by-product, and produces renewable energy, zero waste, and zero wastewater. Billen et al. (2015) take into account that during poultry litter combustion nitrogen is lost as nitrogen gas, as opposed to during direct application to land, and acknowledge the need to, in some cases, apply artificial nitrogen fertilizer to sup- plement the nitrogen need, when poultry litter ash is applied as fertilizer. Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 analyze the characteristics of poultry litter as fuel, describe the different thermal conversion technologies in which it can be utilized, propose measures to in- crease the sustainability of the treatment of poultry litter, and stress the importance of proper use of the ash after thermal conversion and of controlling harmful process emissions. Both studies are limited in scope, as Billen et al. (2015) consider only direct appli- cation of poultry litter and thermal conversion, and Santos Dalólio et al., 2017 only thermal conversion technologies. Neither of these studies takes into account that organic matter contained in the poultry litter increases the soil carbon content (Rice, 2005), and neither study acknowledges that, in addition to nitrogen, in some instances phosphorus and potassium fertilizers must be supplemented.

10: An attributional life cycle assessment (LCA) is conducted. LCA identifies, quantifies, and evaluates the environmental impact of a product, service, or activity. The attributional LCA methodology is usually applied, when different products or services are compared that are already offered on the market, and when supporting micro-level decisions (e.g. the treatment of one tonne of poultry litter) (European Commission, 2010). The use of attributional LCA for use in micro-decision support has been debated by Ekvall et al. (2016), stating that a consequential LCA is the methodology of choice for any-type of decision support. In practice, however, attributional LCA can be used to approximate of the effect of micro- scale decisions because big changes are less likely.

The choice for the attributional LCA method means that all results should be interpreted for the treatment of one tonne of poultry litter and that the effect of large changes in the market for poultry litter treatment (e.g shifting treatment of tens of kilotonnes) are not taken into consideration. In addition, a separate comparison of the benefit provided by the different treatment methods for nutrient manage- ment and effective organic matter is carried out.
This section first describes the different treatment methods considered and then the methodology for the LCA and for estab- lishing the nutrient balance.
11: An attributional life cycle assessment (LCA) is conducted. LCA identifies, quantifies, and evaluates the environmental impact of a product, service, or activity. The attributional LCA methodology is usually applied, when different products or services are compared that are already offered on the market, and when supporting micro-level decisions (e.g. the treatment of one tonne of poultry litter) (European Commission, 2010). (2016), stating that a consequential LCA is the methodology of choice for any-type of decision support. This section first describes the different treatment methods considered and then the methodology for the LCA and for estab- lishing the nutrient balance.
12: Five different treatment methods are considered: direct land application, composting, composting and granulation, co-digestion, and thermal conversion.
13: In the Netherlands, if poultry litter is applied directly to agri- cultural land, it is applied from February to September by means of band spreading, so that the litter is incorporated into the soil. The remainder of the year, direct application of poultry litter is not allowed, so that the poultry litter is stored for later application or is treated by a different treatment method.

Direct application in the Netherlands may in this sense differ from that in other countries because of the Dutch legislation on the method of application. In some other countries, e.g. Germany, litter may be directly applied to land without covering it, which leads to higher direct emissions. The results of direct application in the Netherlands can thus be seen as a best case for direct land appli- cation, other methods likely have a higher environmental impact
14: Poultry litter is composted at several locations in the Netherlands. This study considers a company that composts poultry litter in two composting tunnels. The composter applies both natural composting and forced composting, but in this study natural composting will be considered, as it is more common among companies that compost poultry litter in the Netherlands (e.g. Eraspo, Ferm o Feed, Komeco),. In the case of natural com- posting no electricity or heat is needed for the processing and the compost heaps are aerated by regular turning by means of a tractor. The air from the composting tunnels passes through an air scrubber before it is released into the atmosphere. This air treatment uses electricity.

Composting at the considered composter occurs in combination with other types of feedstock: on average 75 wt% of the input material is poultry litter and 25 wt% is other types of litter/manure or organic waste.

The economically valuable products arising from this treatment method are compost and water from the air scrubber (drain water, which still contains useful nutrients). In general, no NH3 emissions occur during the application of compost (Plant Research International, 2007), but N20 and nitrate are emitted, when compost is applied to agricultural land. Compost produced in the Netherlands is largely applied outside the Netherlands, e.g.in France and Germany. Whether or not application of this type of compost as fertilizer is allowed, depends on the fertilizer legislation in the countries/regions.
15: Poultry litter is composted at several locations in the Netherlands. This study considers a company that composts poultry litter in two composting tunnels. The composter applies both natural composting and forced composting, but in this study natural composting will be considered, as it is more common among companies that compost poultry litter in the Netherlands (e.g. Eraspo, Ferm o Feed, Komeco),. In the case of natural com- posting no electricity or heat is needed for the processing and the compost heaps are aerated by regular turning by means of a tractor. The air from the composting tunnels passes through an air scrubber before it is released into the atmosphere. This air treatment uses electricity.

Composting at the considered composter occurs in combination with other types of feedstock: on average 75 wt% of the input material is poultry litter and 25 wt% is other types of litter/manure or organic waste.

The economically valuable products arising from this treatment method are compost and water from the air scrubber (drain water, which still contains useful nutrients). In general, no NH3 emissions occur during the application of compost (Plant Research International, 2007), but N20 and nitrate are emitted, when compost is applied to agricultural land. Compost produced in the Netherlands is largely applied outside the Netherlands, e.g.in France and Germany. Whether or not application of this type of compost as fertilizer is allowed, depends on the fertilizer legislation in the countries/regions.
16: After composting, the poultry litter is further treated to make it suitable for long-term storage: it is dried and then compacted into a granulate. In the facility considered, a Dutch company produces granulates using a fluidized bed dryer.
17: High nitrogen content makes it difficult to digest poultry litter by itself (Borowski et al., 2016). As often done in Germany, poultry litter may be co-digested with energy crops, usually fodder maize (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). In this study co- digestion of 50 wt% of poultry litter and 50 wt% of fodder maize will be considered.

The first step of the process consists of mixing the poultry litter with the fodder maize. The mixture is then treated by anaerobic digestion, which produces biogas as well as digestate. The biogas, which contains approximately 55 wt% of methane, is generally used in small-scale combined heat and power installations (CHP) pro- ducing electricity. The CHP also produces heat in excess of what is needed in the digestion process. The net heat produced is normally not sold to be used e.g. by industry or district heating, and it is unclear whether it replaces conventional heat produced by means of, for example, natural gas. Therefore, heat is not taken into consideration as valuable product, but only electricity produced from biogas and digestate are considered as economically valuable products arising from the co-digestion process. The digestate can be applied as fertilizer.
18: High nitrogen content makes it difficult to digest poultry litter by itself (Borowski et al., 2016). As often done in Germany, poultry litter may be co-digested with energy crops, usually fodder maize (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). In this study co- digestion of 50 wt% of poultry litter and 50 wt% of fodder maize will be considered.

The first step of the process consists of mixing the poultry litter with the fodder maize. The mixture is then treated by anaerobic digestion, which produces biogas as well as digestate. The biogas, which contains approximately 55 wt% of methane, is generally used in small-scale combined heat and power installations (CHP) pro- ducing electricity. The CHP also produces heat in excess of what is needed in the digestion process. The net heat produced is normally not sold to be used e.g. by industry or district heating, and it is unclear whether it replaces conventional heat produced by means of, for example, natural gas. Therefore, heat is not taken into consideration as valuable product, but only electricity produced from biogas and digestate are considered as economically valuable products arising from the co-digestion process. The digestate can be applied as fertilizer.
19: In the Netherlands, poultry litter is incinerated at BMC Moerdijk, using a fluidized bed combustor (FBC), plant, which generates electricity by means of a steam boiler and a turbine. Extensive off- gas treatment is in place to prevent harmful emissions. Poultry litter is stored indoor and the air from the storage area is used as combustion air, so that no emissions, including odorous emissions,
occur from the storage. An extensive description of the technology applied at BMC is given by Billen et al. (2015).

Economically valuable products arising from this treatment method are electricity, which is fed into the electricity grid, and poultry litter ash. The poultry litter ash from the BMC Moerdijk plant is sold as a fertilizer for use outside the Netherlands, e.g. in Belgium, France, and the UK. Whether or not application of poultry litter ash as fertilizer is allowed, depends on the fertilizer legislation in the countries/regions.
20: In this section the following aspects of LCA will be discussed: scope, inventory analysis, impact assessment, and sensitivity analysis.
21: <header>2.2.1. Scope </header>
Under this heading will be discussed: system boundaries, mul- tifunctionality and functional unit, and allocation.

2.2.1.1. System boundaries. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries as applied in this study. The system boundaries include both the treatment system as well as the expanded system so that all treatment routes deliver the same functions. The description of the approach to multifunctionality and the functional unit is described in Section 2.2.1.2.

To make the results applicable in different countries and with different legislations concerning litter storage, this study does not include litter storage, but focusses on the litter treatment and application. This means that the first life cycle stage taken into consideration within the treatment system is the treatment of the poultry litter. The sensitivity of the results to the exclusion of the litter storage is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

All downstream processes and all produced products from the different treatment methods are included in this study (poultry litter ash, raw poultry litter, poultry litter compost, poultry litter granu- lates, poultry litter digestate and electricity). The nitrogen, phos- phorus, potassium, and organic matter content of the fertilizing products are calculated. All other (micro-) nutrients and heavy metals contained in the fertilizer products are excluded, because the quantities present in the different products per tonne (1000 kg) of poultry litter treated are the same. The concentration of micro- nutrients and heavy metals per product does differ. Since not enough data was available on the concentration to include it in de analysis, it will be discussed qualitatively in the discussion section.

It can be assumed that, if a litter treatment method is applied, the fertilizer products can be applied somewhere. The exact transport distance from the farm to the treatment facility, as well as the transport distance of the fertilizer products can differ, depending on local legislation and market circumstances. Since it is not an attribute of the technologies studied, transportation is not taken into consideration in the basic analysis, but the sensitivity of the conclusions to this exclusion is studied in a sensitivity analysis.

The leaching of phosphate from agricultural soils (after appli- cation of fertilizer products) was not included for any of the end- products, as its extent is indeed governed more by environmental factors like soil saturation and field slope, than by the fertilizer type (Djodjic et al., 2004). Given the complexity of the issue, it was not included in this study. All other emissions are considered.
The impact of production of capital goods/infrastructure is excluded.
2.2.1.2. Multifunctionality and functional unit. This study considers the treatment of 1 tonne of raw poultry litter with the following composition: broiler chicken litter, 40% laying hen litter, 5%

turkey litter and 3% manure-belt litter. A summary of the exact composition as relevant for this study is given in Table 1. The composition represents the average feedstock as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014. As a sampling procedure, three samples were taken per day and combined to one sample. A com- bination of samples per week (so seven samples) is mixed and then analyzed by an ISO17025 accredited laboratory. A total of 58 mixed samples were analyzed.
Subscript Table 1: Composition of 1 tonne of poultry litter as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014.

As the different treatment methods of poultry litter lead to different products and services, this study deals with a multifunc- tional system generating both the service of litter treatment as well as the production of one or multiple products. According to ISO14044, different approaches exist when a system under study has multiple functions: it is preferable to prevent allocation, but allocation based on physical or other relationships is also possible. The most common approaches to prevent allocation are system expansion and substitution (being a subtractive case of system expansion). In substitution the environmental benefit of avoiding production of products in the economy is subtracted from the environmental impact of the production of the functional unit. In system expansion the original functional unit is expanded in such a way that all the compared routes produce the same quantity of products.

The use of substitution in attributional LCA is heavily debated and the ISO standard is not clear on whether it is appropriate. Brander and Wylie (2011) argue that substitution adds conse- quential elements to an attributional LCA, as it is necessary to determine avoided burdens. According to the ILCD Handbook, system expansion and substitution can be used in some instances in attributional LCA (European Commission, 2010). For these rea- sons, this study applies system expansion, the functional unit being chosen to include not only the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter, but all the useful products from the five treatment methods, as shown in Table 2. The quantities indicated in the table are the maximum quantity of the product obtained within one of the treatment routes. Co-digestion leads to the maximum quantity of calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer, whereas thermal treatment leads to the maximum quantity of electricity and of the other two fertilizer types.

2.2.1.3. Allocation. In the treatment of poultry litter by composting, poultry litter is combined with another feedstock. In the case of digestion, poultry litter is co-digested with maize fodder. Co-digestion. As primary data are not available, an estimation is made of the in- and outputs of the treatment method. The quantity of biogas and digestate produced from digestion of poultry litter and fodder maize are determined based on Reinhold (2005). It is assumed that the produced biogas has a methane content of 55%, corresponding to an energy content of MJ/m³, and that the CHP has a net electrical efficiency of 31% (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). Methane emissions during treat- ment are based on Zwart et al. (2006). No other emissions have been taken into consideration.
Emissions to air and water from poultry litter as such or from application to land of the fertilizer products obtained in the treat- ment processes, have been obtained as follows:

<list>Poultry litter ash. No emissions occur during the application of poultry litter ash on land besides potential phosphate leaching, which was not included in the study for any of the treatment methods. 
Direct application. Emissions to air and water have been based on the composition of the raw poultry litter and emission factors from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013). 
Compost. N20 emissions are based on the composition of the compost and the emission factor from van Bruggen et al., 2015. No data has been found for nitrate emissions to water from poultry litter compost; it is assumed that the emission factor for raw poultry litter obtained from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) can be used, since (almost) all nitrogen present in the raw poultry manure is still present in the compost. 
Granulates. No data has been found for emissions from appli- cation of granulates. The same emission factors are assumed as for application of compost, since the only difference between the granulates and compost is the water content and the con- centration of nutrients.</list>

Co-digestion. Ammonia emissions to air and nitrate emissions to water from application of digestate are comparable to those from direct application of litter (per kg nitrogen in the digestate), the N20 emissions can be a little lower (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). 'A little lower' was interpreted as a reduc- tion by 5% of the N20 emissions in comparison to direct application of poultry litter.

CO2 is first taken up from the atmosphere and converted to C by the plants that served as fodder for the poultry and it ends up in the poultry litter. Application to land of the fertilizer products con- taining this carbon will increase the soil carbon content. This sys- tem may be considered as a CO2 sink. Over a period of 100 years a part of the carbon (expressed as % of the carbon present in the fertilizer product) remains in the soil, representing the CO2 sequestration over a period of 100 years. As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). The composition of the fertilizer products can be found in the supple- mentary data under '2.1 fertilizer equivalents'. Decomposition proceeds according to the relationship (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999):
Ct=Cto.e-abckt (1) where Ct is the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time t,

Cto is the quantity of carbon added to the soil at time 0 and k is the speed with which the type of organic material breaks down. The values for a, b and C depend on the exact location where the fer- tilizer products will be used. In this study the factors a, b and C were not used, as it is assumed that all fertilizer products are applied under the same circumstances. This means no allowance was made for differences in the moisture regime in the soils, where the fer- tilizer materials are applied, the soil temperatures in the areas concerned, the nitrogen and phosphorus available for formation of soil organic matter and the aging of the organic matter involved in the decomposition process.

In the Roth-C model biomass is taken to be a combination of readily decomposable plant material (DPM) and material that is less so (Resistant Plant Material, RPM). Both types of plant material are broken down into CO2 that is emitted, microbial biomass (BIO) and humified organic matter (HUM). In Roth-C the ratio of HUM to BIO is set at 46:54. In turn, both these decomposition products (BIO and HUM) are converted to a mixture of CO2, BIO and HUM. The speed at which each of these types of organic matter are broken down are 10 for DPM, 0.3 for RPM, 0.66 for BIO and 0.02 for HUM.
This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):
CO2 (2) (BIO +

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.
22: <header>2.2.1. Scope </header>
Under this heading will be discussed: system boundaries, mul- tifunctionality and functional unit, and allocation.

2.2.1.1. System boundaries. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries as applied in this study. The system boundaries include both the treatment system as well as the expanded system so that all treatment routes deliver the same functions. The description of the approach to multifunctionality and the functional unit is described in Section 2.2.1.2.

To make the results applicable in different countries and with different legislations concerning litter storage, this study does not include litter storage, but focusses on the litter treatment and application. This means that the first life cycle stage taken into consideration within the treatment system is the treatment of the poultry litter. The sensitivity of the results to the exclusion of the litter storage is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

All downstream processes and all produced products from the different treatment methods are included in this study (poultry litter ash, raw poultry litter, poultry litter compost, poultry litter granu- lates, poultry litter digestate and electricity). The nitrogen, phos- phorus, potassium, and organic matter content of the fertilizing products are calculated. All other (micro-) nutrients and heavy metals contained in the fertilizer products are excluded, because the quantities present in the different products per tonne (1000 kg) of poultry litter treated are the same. The concentration of micro- nutrients and heavy metals per product does differ. Since not enough data was available on the concentration to include it in de analysis, it will be discussed qualitatively in the discussion section.

It can be assumed that, if a litter treatment method is applied, the fertilizer products can be applied somewhere. The exact transport distance from the farm to the treatment facility, as well as the transport distance of the fertilizer products can differ, depending on local legislation and market circumstances. Since it is not an attribute of the technologies studied, transportation is not taken into consideration in the basic analysis, but the sensitivity of the conclusions to this exclusion is studied in a sensitivity analysis.

The leaching of phosphate from agricultural soils (after appli- cation of fertilizer products) was not included for any of the end- products, as its extent is indeed governed more by environmental factors like soil saturation and field slope, than by the fertilizer type (Djodjic et al., 2004). Given the complexity of the issue, it was not included in this study. All other emissions are considered.
The impact of production of capital goods/infrastructure is excluded.

The composition represents the average feedstock as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014. As a sampling procedure, three samples were taken per day and combined to one sample. A com- bination of samples per week (so seven samples) is mixed and then analyzed by an ISO17025 accredited laboratory. A total of 58 mixed samples were analyzed.
Subscript Table 1: Composition of 1 tonne of poultry litter as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014.

As the different treatment methods of poultry litter lead to different products and services, this study deals with a multifunc- tional system generating both the service of litter treatment as well as the production of one or multiple products. According to ISO14044, different approaches exist when a system under study has multiple functions: it is preferable to prevent allocation, but allocation based on physical or other relationships is also possible. The most common approaches to prevent allocation are system expansion and substitution (being a subtractive case of system expansion). In substitution the environmental benefit of avoiding production of products in the economy is subtracted from the environmental impact of the production of the functional unit. In system expansion the original functional unit is expanded in such a way that all the compared routes produce the same quantity of products.

The use of substitution in attributional LCA is heavily debated and the ISO standard is not clear on whether it is appropriate. Brander and Wylie (2011) argue that substitution adds conse- quential elements to an attributional LCA, as it is necessary to determine avoided burdens. According to the ILCD Handbook, system expansion and substitution can be used in some instances in attributional LCA (European Commission, 2010). For these rea- sons, this study applies system expansion, the functional unit being chosen to include not only the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter, but all the useful products from the five treatment methods, as shown in Table 2. The quantities indicated in the table are the maximum quantity of the product obtained within one of the treatment routes. Co-digestion leads to the maximum quantity of calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer, whereas thermal treatment leads to the maximum quantity of electricity and of the other two fertilizer types.

As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). The composition of the fertilizer products can be found in the supple- mentary data under '2.1 fertilizer equivalents'. Decomposition proceeds according to the relationship (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999):
Ct=Cto.e-abckt (1) where Ct is the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time t,

Cto is the quantity of carbon added to the soil at time 0 and k is the speed with which the type of organic material breaks down. The values for a, b and C depend on the exact location where the fer- tilizer products will be used. In this study the factors a, b and C were not used, as it is assumed that all fertilizer products are applied under the same circumstances. This means no allowance was made for differences in the moisture regime in the soils, where the fer- tilizer materials are applied, the soil temperatures in the areas concerned, the nitrogen and phosphorus available for formation of soil organic matter and the aging of the organic matter involved in the decomposition process.

In the Roth-C model biomass is taken to be a combination of readily decomposable plant material (DPM) and material that is less so (Resistant Plant Material, RPM). Both types of plant material are broken down into CO2 that is emitted, microbial biomass (BIO) and humified organic matter (HUM). In Roth-C the ratio of HUM to BIO is set at 46:54. In turn, both these decomposition products (BIO and HUM) are converted to a mixture of CO2, BIO and HUM. The speed at which each of these types of organic matter are broken down are 10 for DPM, 0.3 for RPM, 0.66 for BIO and 0.02 for HUM.
This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):
CO2 (2) (BIO +

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.
23: <header>2.2.1. Scope </header>
Under this heading will be discussed: system boundaries, mul- tifunctionality and functional unit, and allocation.

2.2.1.1. System boundaries. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries as applied in this study. The system boundaries include both the treatment system as well as the expanded system so that all treatment routes deliver the same functions. The description of the approach to multifunctionality and the functional unit is described in Section 2.2.1.2.

To make the results applicable in different countries and with different legislations concerning litter storage, this study does not include litter storage, but focusses on the litter treatment and application. This means that the first life cycle stage taken into consideration within the treatment system is the treatment of the poultry litter. The sensitivity of the results to the exclusion of the litter storage is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

All downstream processes and all produced products from the different treatment methods are included in this study (poultry litter ash, raw poultry litter, poultry litter compost, poultry litter granu- lates, poultry litter digestate and electricity). The nitrogen, phos- phorus, potassium, and organic matter content of the fertilizing products are calculated. All other (micro-) nutrients and heavy metals contained in the fertilizer products are excluded, because the quantities present in the different products per tonne (1000 kg) of poultry litter treated are the same. The concentration of micro- nutrients and heavy metals per product does differ. Since not enough data was available on the concentration to include it in de analysis, it will be discussed qualitatively in the discussion section.

It can be assumed that, if a litter treatment method is applied, the fertilizer products can be applied somewhere. The exact transport distance from the farm to the treatment facility, as well as the transport distance of the fertilizer products can differ, depending on local legislation and market circumstances. Since it is not an attribute of the technologies studied, transportation is not taken into consideration in the basic analysis, but the sensitivity of the conclusions to this exclusion is studied in a sensitivity analysis.

The leaching of phosphate from agricultural soils (after appli- cation of fertilizer products) was not included for any of the end- products, as its extent is indeed governed more by environmental factors like soil saturation and field slope, than by the fertilizer type (Djodjic et al., 2004). Given the complexity of the issue, it was not included in this study. All other emissions are considered.
The impact of production of capital goods/infrastructure is excluded.
2.2.1.2. Multifunctionality and functional unit. This study considers the treatment of 1 tonne of raw poultry litter with the following composition: broiler chicken litter, 40% laying hen litter, 5%

turkey litter and 3% manure-belt litter. A summary of the exact composition as relevant for this study is given in Table 1. The composition represents the average feedstock as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014. As a sampling procedure, three samples were taken per day and combined to one sample. A com- bination of samples per week (so seven samples) is mixed and then analyzed by an ISO17025 accredited laboratory. A total of 58 mixed samples were analyzed.
Subscript Table 1: Composition of 1 tonne of poultry litter as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014.

As the different treatment methods of poultry litter lead to different products and services, this study deals with a multifunc- tional system generating both the service of litter treatment as well as the production of one or multiple products. According to ISO14044, different approaches exist when a system under study has multiple functions: it is preferable to prevent allocation, but allocation based on physical or other relationships is also possible. The most common approaches to prevent allocation are system expansion and substitution (being a subtractive case of system expansion). In substitution the environmental benefit of avoiding production of products in the economy is subtracted from the environmental impact of the production of the functional unit. In system expansion the original functional unit is expanded in such a way that all the compared routes produce the same quantity of products.

The use of substitution in attributional LCA is heavily debated and the ISO standard is not clear on whether it is appropriate. Brander and Wylie (2011) argue that substitution adds conse- quential elements to an attributional LCA, as it is necessary to determine avoided burdens. According to the ILCD Handbook, system expansion and substitution can be used in some instances in attributional LCA (European Commission, 2010). For these rea- sons, this study applies system expansion, the functional unit being chosen to include not only the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter, but all the useful products from the five treatment methods, as shown in Table 2. The quantities indicated in the table are the maximum quantity of the product obtained within one of the treatment routes. Co-digestion leads to the maximum quantity of calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer, whereas thermal treatment leads to the maximum quantity of electricity and of the other two fertilizer types.

2.2.1.3. Allocation. In the treatment of poultry litter by composting, poultry litter is combined with another feedstock. In the case of digestion, poultry litter is co-digested with maize fodder. For both treatment routes (composting and co-digestion) the inputs, prod- ucts, and emissions are allocated based on the mass of the co- treated feedstock.
2.2.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI)
In this section data use and assumptions will be discussed and data quality will be assessed.
2.2.2.1. Data use and assumptions. In this section an overview is given of the overall data used and of the assumptions made. The life cycle inventory is given in Table 3.
Data on auxiliaries and emissions to air for the different treat- ment methods has been obtained as follows:

<list>Thermal conversion. BMC Moerdijk has good data on the aux- iliaries used in the process, the quantity of electricity and wastes produced, as well as on emissions from the thermal conversion process. This data over 2015 is included in the LCI. Greenhouse gas emissions from the process have been calculated based on CO2 emission factors for diesel and natural gas use. The CO2 emissions from the combustion of poultry litter are biogenic and are therefore not included in the results on climate change impact. 
Composting. Data on energy use, auxiliary use, and emissions from the composting process as applied in 2015 have been ob- tained from an anonymous Dutch composter in 2016. 
Composting and granulation. Data on energy use, auxiliary use and emissions from the composting and granulation process as applied in 2015 has been obtained from a composting and granulation company in the Netherlands in 2016.</list>

Co-digestion. As primary data are not available, an estimation is made of the in- and outputs of the treatment method. The quantity of biogas and digestate produced from digestion of poultry litter and fodder maize are determined based on Reinhold (2005). It is assumed that the produced biogas has a methane content of 55%, corresponding to an energy content of MJ/m³, and that the CHP has a net electrical efficiency of 31% (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). Methane emissions during treat- ment are based on Zwart et al. (2006). No other emissions have been taken into consideration.
Emissions to air and water from poultry litter as such or from application to land of the fertilizer products obtained in the treat- ment processes, have been obtained as follows:

<list>Poultry litter ash. No emissions occur during the application of poultry litter ash on land besides potential phosphate leaching, which was not included in the study for any of the treatment methods. 
Direct application. Emissions to air and water have been based on the composition of the raw poultry litter and emission factors from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013). 
Compost. N20 emissions are based on the composition of the compost and the emission factor from van Bruggen et al., 2015. No data has been found for nitrate emissions to water from poultry litter compost; it is assumed that the emission factor for raw poultry litter obtained from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) can be used, since (almost) all nitrogen present in the raw poultry manure is still present in the compost. 
Granulates. No data has been found for emissions from appli- cation of granulates. The same emission factors are assumed as for application of compost, since the only difference between the granulates and compost is the water content and the con- centration of nutrients.</list>

Co-digestion. Ammonia emissions to air and nitrate emissions to water from application of digestate are comparable to those from direct application of litter (per kg nitrogen in the digestate), the N20 emissions can be a little lower (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). 'A little lower' was interpreted as a reduc- tion by 5% of the N20 emissions in comparison to direct application of poultry litter.

CO2 is first taken up from the atmosphere and converted to C by the plants that served as fodder for the poultry and it ends up in the poultry litter. Application to land of the fertilizer products con- taining this carbon will increase the soil carbon content. This sys- tem may be considered as a CO2 sink. Over a period of 100 years a part of the carbon (expressed as % of the carbon present in the fertilizer product) remains in the soil, representing the CO2 sequestration over a period of 100 years. As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.

In this section data use and assumptions will be discussed and data quality will be assessed.

Co-digestion. As primary data are not available, an estimation is made of the in- and outputs of the treatment method. The quantity of biogas and digestate produced from digestion of poultry litter and fodder maize are determined based on Reinhold (2005). It is assumed that the produced biogas has a methane content of 55%, corresponding to an energy content of MJ/m³, and that the CHP has a net electrical efficiency of 31% (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). Methane emissions during treat- ment are based on Zwart et al. (2006). No other emissions have been taken into consideration.
Emissions to air and water from poultry litter as such or from application to land of the fertilizer products obtained in the treat- ment processes, have been obtained as follows:

<list>Poultry litter ash. No emissions occur during the application of poultry litter ash on land besides potential phosphate leaching, which was not included in the study for any of the treatment methods. 
Direct application. Emissions to air and water have been based on the composition of the raw poultry litter and emission factors from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013). 
Compost. N20 emissions are based on the composition of the compost and the emission factor from van Bruggen et al., 2015. No data has been found for nitrate emissions to water from poultry litter compost; it is assumed that the emission factor for raw poultry litter obtained from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) can be used, since (almost) all nitrogen present in the raw poultry manure is still present in the compost. 
Granulates. No data has been found for emissions from appli- cation of granulates. The same emission factors are assumed as for application of compost, since the only difference between the granulates and compost is the water content and the con- centration of nutrients.</list>

Co-digestion. Ammonia emissions to air and nitrate emissions to water from application of digestate are comparable to those from direct application of litter (per kg nitrogen in the digestate), the N20 emissions can be a little lower (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). 'A little lower' was interpreted as a reduc- tion by 5% of the N20 emissions in comparison to direct application of poultry litter.

CO2 is first taken up from the atmosphere and converted to C by the plants that served as fodder for the poultry and it ends up in the poultry litter. Application to land of the fertilizer products con- taining this carbon will increase the soil carbon content. This sys- tem may be considered as a CO2 sink. Over a period of 100 years a part of the carbon (expressed as % of the carbon present in the fertilizer product) remains in the soil, representing the CO2 sequestration over a period of 100 years. As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). The composition of the fertilizer products can be found in the supple- mentary data under '2.1 fertilizer equivalents'. Decomposition proceeds according to the relationship (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999):
Ct=Cto.e-abckt (1) where Ct is the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time t,

Cto is the quantity of carbon added to the soil at time 0 and k is the speed with which the type of organic material breaks down. The values for a, b and C depend on the exact location where the fer- tilizer products will be used. In this study the factors a, b and C were not used, as it is assumed that all fertilizer products are applied under the same circumstances. This means no allowance was made for differences in the moisture regime in the soils, where the fer- tilizer materials are applied, the soil temperatures in the areas concerned, the nitrogen and phosphorus available for formation of soil organic matter and the aging of the organic matter involved in the decomposition process.

In the Roth-C model biomass is taken to be a combination of readily decomposable plant material (DPM) and material that is less so (Resistant Plant Material, RPM). Both types of plant material are broken down into CO2 that is emitted, microbial biomass (BIO) and humified organic matter (HUM). In Roth-C the ratio of HUM to BIO is set at 46:54. In turn, both these decomposition products (BIO and HUM) are converted to a mixture of CO2, BIO and HUM. The speed at which each of these types of organic matter are broken down are 10 for DPM, 0.3 for RPM, 0.66 for BIO and 0.02 for HUM.
This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):
CO2 (2) (BIO +

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.
25: <header>2.2.1. Scope </header>
Under this heading will be discussed: system boundaries, mul- tifunctionality and functional unit, and allocation.

2.2.1.1. System boundaries. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries as applied in this study. The system boundaries include both the treatment system as well as the expanded system so that all treatment routes deliver the same functions. The description of the approach to multifunctionality and the functional unit is described in Section 2.2.1.2.

To make the results applicable in different countries and with different legislations concerning litter storage, this study does not include litter storage, but focusses on the litter treatment and application. This means that the first life cycle stage taken into consideration within the treatment system is the treatment of the poultry litter. The sensitivity of the results to the exclusion of the litter storage is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

All downstream processes and all produced products from the different treatment methods are included in this study (poultry litter ash, raw poultry litter, poultry litter compost, poultry litter granu- lates, poultry litter digestate and electricity). The nitrogen, phos- phorus, potassium, and organic matter content of the fertilizing products are calculated. All other (micro-) nutrients and heavy metals contained in the fertilizer products are excluded, because the quantities present in the different products per tonne (1000 kg) of poultry litter treated are the same. The concentration of micro- nutrients and heavy metals per product does differ. Since not enough data was available on the concentration to include it in de analysis, it will be discussed qualitatively in the discussion section.

It can be assumed that, if a litter treatment method is applied, the fertilizer products can be applied somewhere. The exact transport distance from the farm to the treatment facility, as well as the transport distance of the fertilizer products can differ, depending on local legislation and market circumstances. Since it is not an attribute of the technologies studied, transportation is not taken into consideration in the basic analysis, but the sensitivity of the conclusions to this exclusion is studied in a sensitivity analysis.

The leaching of phosphate from agricultural soils (after appli- cation of fertilizer products) was not included for any of the end- products, as its extent is indeed governed more by environmental factors like soil saturation and field slope, than by the fertilizer type (Djodjic et al., 2004). Given the complexity of the issue, it was not included in this study. All other emissions are considered.
The impact of production of capital goods/infrastructure is excluded.

turkey litter and 3% manure-belt litter. A summary of the exact composition as relevant for this study is given in Table 1. The composition represents the average feedstock as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014. As a sampling procedure, three samples were taken per day and combined to one sample. A com- bination of samples per week (so seven samples) is mixed and then analyzed by an ISO17025 accredited laboratory. A total of 58 mixed samples were analyzed.
Subscript Table 1: Composition of 1 tonne of poultry litter as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014.

As the different treatment methods of poultry litter lead to different products and services, this study deals with a multifunc- tional system generating both the service of litter treatment as well as the production of one or multiple products. According to ISO14044, different approaches exist when a system under study has multiple functions: it is preferable to prevent allocation, but allocation based on physical or other relationships is also possible. The most common approaches to prevent allocation are system expansion and substitution (being a subtractive case of system expansion). In substitution the environmental benefit of avoiding production of products in the economy is subtracted from the environmental impact of the production of the functional unit. In system expansion the original functional unit is expanded in such a way that all the compared routes produce the same quantity of products.

The use of substitution in attributional LCA is heavily debated and the ISO standard is not clear on whether it is appropriate. Brander and Wylie (2011) argue that substitution adds conse- quential elements to an attributional LCA, as it is necessary to determine avoided burdens. According to the ILCD Handbook, system expansion and substitution can be used in some instances in attributional LCA (European Commission, 2010). For these rea- sons, this study applies system expansion, the functional unit being chosen to include not only the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter, but all the useful products from the five treatment methods, as shown in Table 2. The quantities indicated in the table are the maximum quantity of the product obtained within one of the treatment routes. Co-digestion leads to the maximum quantity of calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer, whereas thermal treatment leads to the maximum quantity of electricity and of the other two fertilizer types.

2.2.1.3. Allocation. In the treatment of poultry litter by composting, poultry litter is combined with another feedstock. In the case of digestion, poultry litter is co-digested with maize fodder. For both treatment routes (composting and co-digestion) the inputs, prod- ucts, and emissions are allocated based on the mass of the co- treated feedstock.
