Co-digestion. As primary data are not available, an estimation is made of the in- and outputs of the treatment method. The quantity of biogas and digestate produced from digestion of poultry litter and fodder maize are determined based on Reinhold (2005). It is assumed that the produced biogas has a methane content of 55%, corresponding to an energy content of MJ/m³, and that the CHP has a net electrical efficiency of 31% (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). Methane emissions during treat- ment are based on Zwart et al. (2006). No other emissions have been taken into consideration.
Emissions to air and water from poultry litter as such or from application to land of the fertilizer products obtained in the treat- ment processes, have been obtained as follows:

<list>Poultry litter ash. No emissions occur during the application of poultry litter ash on land besides potential phosphate leaching, which was not included in the study for any of the treatment methods. 
Direct application. Emissions to air and water have been based on the composition of the raw poultry litter and emission factors from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013). 
Compost. N20 emissions are based on the composition of the compost and the emission factor from van Bruggen et al., 2015. No data has been found for nitrate emissions to water from poultry litter compost; it is assumed that the emission factor for raw poultry litter obtained from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) can be used, since (almost) all nitrogen present in the raw poultry manure is still present in the compost. 
Granulates. No data has been found for emissions from appli- cation of granulates. The same emission factors are assumed as for application of compost, since the only difference between the granulates and compost is the water content and the con- centration of nutrients.</list>

Co-digestion. Ammonia emissions to air and nitrate emissions to water from application of digestate are comparable to those from direct application of litter (per kg nitrogen in the digestate), the N20 emissions can be a little lower (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). 'A little lower' was interpreted as a reduc- tion by 5% of the N20 emissions in comparison to direct application of poultry litter.

CO2 is first taken up from the atmosphere and converted to C by the plants that served as fodder for the poultry and it ends up in the poultry litter. Application to land of the fertilizer products con- taining this carbon will increase the soil carbon content. This sys- tem may be considered as a CO2 sink. Over a period of 100 years a part of the carbon (expressed as % of the carbon present in the fertilizer product) remains in the soil, representing the CO2 sequestration over a period of 100 years. As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). The composition of the fertilizer products can be found in the supple- mentary data under '2.1 fertilizer equivalents'. Decomposition proceeds according to the relationship (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999):
Ct=Cto.e-abckt (1) where Ct is the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time t,

Cto is the quantity of carbon added to the soil at time 0 and k is the speed with which the type of organic material breaks down. The values for a, b and C depend on the exact location where the fer- tilizer products will be used. In this study the factors a, b and C were not used, as it is assumed that all fertilizer products are applied under the same circumstances. This means no allowance was made for differences in the moisture regime in the soils, where the fer- tilizer materials are applied, the soil temperatures in the areas concerned, the nitrogen and phosphorus available for formation of soil organic matter and the aging of the organic matter involved in the decomposition process.

In the Roth-C model biomass is taken to be a combination of readily decomposable plant material (DPM) and material that is less so (Resistant Plant Material, RPM). Both types of plant material are broken down into CO2 that is emitted, microbial biomass (BIO) and humified organic matter (HUM). In Roth-C the ratio of HUM to BIO is set at 46:54. In turn, both these decomposition products (BIO and HUM) are converted to a mixture of CO2, BIO and HUM. The speed at which each of these types of organic matter are broken down are 10 for DPM, 0.3 for RPM, 0.66 for BIO and 0.02 for HUM.
This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):
CO2 (2) (BIO +

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.

Co-digestion. As primary data are not available, an estimation is made of the in- and outputs of the treatment method. The quantity of biogas and digestate produced from digestion of poultry litter and fodder maize are determined based on Reinhold (2005). It is assumed that the produced biogas has a methane content of 55%, corresponding to an energy content of MJ/m³, and that the CHP has a net electrical efficiency of 31% (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). Methane emissions during treat- ment are based on Zwart et al. (2006). No other emissions have been taken into consideration.
Emissions to air and water from poultry litter as such or from application to land of the fertilizer products obtained in the treat- ment processes, have been obtained as follows:

<list>Poultry litter ash. No emissions occur during the application of poultry litter ash on land besides potential phosphate leaching, which was not included in the study for any of the treatment methods. 
Direct application. Emissions to air and water have been based on the composition of the raw poultry litter and emission factors from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013). 
Compost. N20 emissions are based on the composition of the compost and the emission factor from van Bruggen et al., 2015. No data has been found for nitrate emissions to water from poultry litter compost; it is assumed that the emission factor for raw poultry litter obtained from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) can be used, since (almost) all nitrogen present in the raw poultry manure is still present in the compost. 
Granulates. No data has been found for emissions from appli- cation of granulates. The same emission factors are assumed as for application of compost, since the only difference between the granulates and compost is the water content and the con- centration of nutrients.</list>

Co-digestion. Ammonia emissions to air and nitrate emissions to water from application of digestate are comparable to those from direct application of litter (per kg nitrogen in the digestate), the N20 emissions can be a little lower (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). 'A little lower' was interpreted as a reduc- tion by 5% of the N20 emissions in comparison to direct application of poultry litter.

CO2 is first taken up from the atmosphere and converted to C by the plants that served as fodder for the poultry and it ends up in the poultry litter. Application to land of the fertilizer products con- taining this carbon will increase the soil carbon content. This sys- tem may be considered as a CO2 sink. Over a period of 100 years a part of the carbon (expressed as % of the carbon present in the fertilizer product) remains in the soil, representing the CO2 sequestration over a period of 100 years. As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). The composition of the fertilizer products can be found in the supple- mentary data under '2.1 fertilizer equivalents'. Decomposition proceeds according to the relationship (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999):
Ct=Cto.e-abckt (1) where Ct is the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time t,

Cto is the quantity of carbon added to the soil at time 0 and k is the speed with which the type of organic material breaks down. The values for a, b and C depend on the exact location where the fer- tilizer products will be used. In this study the factors a, b and C were not used, as it is assumed that all fertilizer products are applied under the same circumstances. This means no allowance was made for differences in the moisture regime in the soils, where the fer- tilizer materials are applied, the soil temperatures in the areas concerned, the nitrogen and phosphorus available for formation of soil organic matter and the aging of the organic matter involved in the decomposition process.

In the Roth-C model biomass is taken to be a combination of readily decomposable plant material (DPM) and material that is less so (Resistant Plant Material, RPM). Both types of plant material are broken down into CO2 that is emitted, microbial biomass (BIO) and humified organic matter (HUM). In Roth-C the ratio of HUM to BIO is set at 46:54. In turn, both these decomposition products (BIO and HUM) are converted to a mixture of CO2, BIO and HUM. The speed at which each of these types of organic matter are broken down are 10 for DPM, 0.3 for RPM, 0.66 for BIO and 0.02 for HUM.
This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):
CO2 (2) (BIO +

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.
43: <header>2.2.1. Scope </header>
Under this heading will be discussed: system boundaries, mul- tifunctionality and functional unit, and allocation.

2.2.1.1. System boundaries. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries as applied in this study. The system boundaries include both the treatment system as well as the expanded system so that all treatment routes deliver the same functions. The description of the approach to multifunctionality and the functional unit is described in Section 2.2.1.2.

To make the results applicable in different countries and with different legislations concerning litter storage, this study does not include litter storage, but focusses on the litter treatment and application. This means that the first life cycle stage taken into consideration within the treatment system is the treatment of the poultry litter. The sensitivity of the results to the exclusion of the litter storage is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

All downstream processes and all produced products from the different treatment methods are included in this study (poultry litter ash, raw poultry litter, poultry litter compost, poultry litter granu- lates, poultry litter digestate and electricity). The nitrogen, phos- phorus, potassium, and organic matter content of the fertilizing products are calculated. All other (micro-) nutrients and heavy metals contained in the fertilizer products are excluded, because the quantities present in the different products per tonne (1000 kg) of poultry litter treated are the same. The concentration of micro- nutrients and heavy metals per product does differ. Since not enough data was available on the concentration to include it in de analysis, it will be discussed qualitatively in the discussion section.

It can be assumed that, if a litter treatment method is applied, the fertilizer products can be applied somewhere. The exact transport distance from the farm to the treatment facility, as well as the transport distance of the fertilizer products can differ, depending on local legislation and market circumstances. Since it is not an attribute of the technologies studied, transportation is not taken into consideration in the basic analysis, but the sensitivity of the conclusions to this exclusion is studied in a sensitivity analysis.

The leaching of phosphate from agricultural soils (after appli- cation of fertilizer products) was not included for any of the end- products, as its extent is indeed governed more by environmental factors like soil saturation and field slope, than by the fertilizer type (Djodjic et al., 2004). Given the complexity of the issue, it was not included in this study. All other emissions are considered.
The impact of production of capital goods/infrastructure is excluded.

turkey litter and 3% manure-belt litter. A summary of the exact composition as relevant for this study is given in Table 1. The composition represents the average feedstock as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014. As a sampling procedure, three samples were taken per day and combined to one sample. A com- bination of samples per week (so seven samples) is mixed and then analyzed by an ISO17025 accredited laboratory. A total of 58 mixed samples were analyzed.
Subscript Table 1: Composition of 1 tonne of poultry litter as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014.

As the different treatment methods of poultry litter lead to different products and services, this study deals with a multifunc- tional system generating both the service of litter treatment as well as the production of one or multiple products. According to ISO14044, different approaches exist when a system under study has multiple functions: it is preferable to prevent allocation, but allocation based on physical or other relationships is also possible. The most common approaches to prevent allocation are system expansion and substitution (being a subtractive case of system expansion). In substitution the environmental benefit of avoiding production of products in the economy is subtracted from the environmental impact of the production of the functional unit. In system expansion the original functional unit is expanded in such a way that all the compared routes produce the same quantity of products.

The use of substitution in attributional LCA is heavily debated and the ISO standard is not clear on whether it is appropriate. Brander and Wylie (2011) argue that substitution adds conse- quential elements to an attributional LCA, as it is necessary to determine avoided burdens. According to the ILCD Handbook, system expansion and substitution can be used in some instances in attributional LCA (European Commission, 2010). For these rea- sons, this study applies system expansion, the functional unit being chosen to include not only the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter, but all the useful products from the five treatment methods, as shown in Table 2. The quantities indicated in the table are the maximum quantity of the product obtained within one of the treatment routes. Co-digestion leads to the maximum quantity of calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer, whereas thermal treatment leads to the maximum quantity of electricity and of the other two fertilizer types.

2.2.1.3. Allocation. In the treatment of poultry litter by composting, poultry litter is combined with another feedstock. In the case of digestion, poultry litter is co-digested with maize fodder. For both treatment routes (composting and co-digestion) the inputs, prod- ucts, and emissions are allocated based on the mass of the co- treated feedstock.
2.2.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI)
In this section data use and assumptions will be discussed and data quality will be assessed.
2.2.2.1. Data use and assumptions. In this section an overview is given of the overall data used and of the assumptions made. The life cycle inventory is given in Table 3.

The CO2 emissions from the combustion of poultry litter are biogenic and are therefore not included in the results on climate change impact.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.
44: <header>2.2.1. Scope </header>
Under this heading will be discussed: system boundaries, mul- tifunctionality and functional unit, and allocation.

2.2.1.1. System boundaries. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries as applied in this study. The system boundaries include both the treatment system as well as the expanded system so that all treatment routes deliver the same functions. The description of the approach to multifunctionality and the functional unit is described in Section 2.2.1.2.

To make the results applicable in different countries and with different legislations concerning litter storage, this study does not include litter storage, but focusses on the litter treatment and application. This means that the first life cycle stage taken into consideration within the treatment system is the treatment of the poultry litter. The sensitivity of the results to the exclusion of the litter storage is considered in a sensitivity analysis.

All downstream processes and all produced products from the different treatment methods are included in this study (poultry litter ash, raw poultry litter, poultry litter compost, poultry litter granu- lates, poultry litter digestate and electricity). The nitrogen, phos- phorus, potassium, and organic matter content of the fertilizing products are calculated. All other (micro-) nutrients and heavy metals contained in the fertilizer products are excluded, because the quantities present in the different products per tonne (1000 kg) of poultry litter treated are the same. The concentration of micro- nutrients and heavy metals per product does differ. Since not enough data was available on the concentration to include it in de analysis, it will be discussed qualitatively in the discussion section.

It can be assumed that, if a litter treatment method is applied, the fertilizer products can be applied somewhere. The exact transport distance from the farm to the treatment facility, as well as the transport distance of the fertilizer products can differ, depending on local legislation and market circumstances. Since it is not an attribute of the technologies studied, transportation is not taken into consideration in the basic analysis, but the sensitivity of the conclusions to this exclusion is studied in a sensitivity analysis.

The leaching of phosphate from agricultural soils (after appli- cation of fertilizer products) was not included for any of the end- products, as its extent is indeed governed more by environmental factors like soil saturation and field slope, than by the fertilizer type (Djodjic et al., 2004). Given the complexity of the issue, it was not included in this study. All other emissions are considered.
The impact of production of capital goods/infrastructure is excluded.

turkey litter and 3% manure-belt litter. A summary of the exact composition as relevant for this study is given in Table 1. The composition represents the average feedstock as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014. As a sampling procedure, three samples were taken per day and combined to one sample. A com- bination of samples per week (so seven samples) is mixed and then analyzed by an ISO17025 accredited laboratory. A total of 58 mixed samples were analyzed.
Subscript Table 1: Composition of 1 tonne of poultry litter as treated by BMC Moerdijk between 2012 and 2014.

As the different treatment methods of poultry litter lead to different products and services, this study deals with a multifunc- tional system generating both the service of litter treatment as well as the production of one or multiple products. According to ISO14044, different approaches exist when a system under study has multiple functions: it is preferable to prevent allocation, but allocation based on physical or other relationships is also possible. The most common approaches to prevent allocation are system expansion and substitution (being a subtractive case of system expansion). In substitution the environmental benefit of avoiding production of products in the economy is subtracted from the environmental impact of the production of the functional unit. In system expansion the original functional unit is expanded in such a way that all the compared routes produce the same quantity of products.

The use of substitution in attributional LCA is heavily debated and the ISO standard is not clear on whether it is appropriate. Brander and Wylie (2011) argue that substitution adds conse- quential elements to an attributional LCA, as it is necessary to determine avoided burdens. According to the ILCD Handbook, system expansion and substitution can be used in some instances in attributional LCA (European Commission, 2010). For these rea- sons, this study applies system expansion, the functional unit being chosen to include not only the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter, but all the useful products from the five treatment methods, as shown in Table 2. The quantities indicated in the table are the maximum quantity of the product obtained within one of the treatment routes. Co-digestion leads to the maximum quantity of calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer, whereas thermal treatment leads to the maximum quantity of electricity and of the other two fertilizer types.

2.2.1.3. Allocation. In the treatment of poultry litter by composting, poultry litter is combined with another feedstock. In the case of digestion, poultry litter is co-digested with maize fodder. For both treatment routes (composting and co-digestion) the inputs, prod- ucts, and emissions are allocated based on the mass of the co- treated feedstock.
2.2.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI)
In this section data use and assumptions will be discussed and data quality will be assessed.
2.2.2.1. Data use and assumptions. In this section an overview is given of the overall data used and of the assumptions made. The life cycle inventory is given in Table 3.
Data on auxiliaries and emissions to air for the different treat- ment methods has been obtained as follows:

<list>Thermal conversion. BMC Moerdijk has good data on the aux- iliaries used in the process, the quantity of electricity and wastes produced, as well as on emissions from the thermal conversion process. This data over 2015 is included in the LCI. Greenhouse gas emissions from the process have been calculated based on CO2 emission factors for diesel and natural gas use. The CO2 emissions from the combustion of poultry litter are biogenic and are therefore not included in the results on climate change impact. 
Composting. Data on energy use, auxiliary use, and emissions from the composting process as applied in 2015 have been ob- tained from an anonymous Dutch composter in 2016. 
Composting and granulation. Data on energy use, auxiliary use and emissions from the composting and granulation process as applied in 2015 has been obtained from a composting and granulation company in the Netherlands in 2016.</list>

Co-digestion. As primary data are not available, an estimation is made of the in- and outputs of the treatment method. The quantity of biogas and digestate produced from digestion of poultry litter and fodder maize are determined based on Reinhold (2005). It is assumed that the produced biogas has a methane content of 55%, corresponding to an energy content of MJ/m³, and that the CHP has a net electrical efficiency of 31% (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). Methane emissions during treat- ment are based on Zwart et al. (2006). No other emissions have been taken into consideration.
Emissions to air and water from poultry litter as such or from application to land of the fertilizer products obtained in the treat- ment processes, have been obtained as follows:

<list>Poultry litter ash. No emissions occur during the application of poultry litter ash on land besides potential phosphate leaching, which was not included in the study for any of the treatment methods. 
Direct application. Emissions to air and water have been based on the composition of the raw poultry litter and emission factors from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013). 
Compost. N20 emissions are based on the composition of the compost and the emission factor from van Bruggen et al., 2015. No data has been found for nitrate emissions to water from poultry litter compost; it is assumed that the emission factor for raw poultry litter obtained from Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) can be used, since (almost) all nitrogen present in the raw poultry manure is still present in the compost. 
Granulates. No data has been found for emissions from appli- cation of granulates. The same emission factors are assumed as for application of compost, since the only difference between the granulates and compost is the water content and the con- centration of nutrients.</list>

Co-digestion. Ammonia emissions to air and nitrate emissions to water from application of digestate are comparable to those from direct application of litter (per kg nitrogen in the digestate), the N20 emissions can be a little lower (Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet, 2015). 'A little lower' was interpreted as a reduc- tion by 5% of the N20 emissions in comparison to direct application of poultry litter.

CO2 is first taken up from the atmosphere and converted to C by the plants that served as fodder for the poultry and it ends up in the poultry litter. Application to land of the fertilizer products con- taining this carbon will increase the soil carbon content. This sys- tem may be considered as a CO2 sink. Over a period of 100 years a part of the carbon (expressed as % of the carbon present in the fertilizer product) remains in the soil, representing the CO2 sequestration over a period of 100 years. As there is a difference between the quantity of carbon added to the soil under different treatment routes and therefore also between the CO2 sequestration, this aspect will be taken into consideration.

CO2 sequestration from fertilizer products applied to land has been determined based on the composition of the fertilizer prod- ucts and the Roth-C model (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999). The composition of the fertilizer products can be found in the supple- mentary data under '2.1 fertilizer equivalents'. Decomposition proceeds according to the relationship (Coleman and Jenkinson, 1999):
Ct=Cto.e-abckt (1) where Ct is the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time t,

Cto is the quantity of carbon added to the soil at time 0 and k is the speed with which the type of organic material breaks down. The values for a, b and C depend on the exact location where the fer- tilizer products will be used. In this study the factors a, b and C were not used, as it is assumed that all fertilizer products are applied under the same circumstances. This means no allowance was made for differences in the moisture regime in the soils, where the fer- tilizer materials are applied, the soil temperatures in the areas concerned, the nitrogen and phosphorus available for formation of soil organic matter and the aging of the organic matter involved in the decomposition process.

In the Roth-C model biomass is taken to be a combination of readily decomposable plant material (DPM) and material that is less so (Resistant Plant Material, RPM). Both types of plant material are broken down into CO2 that is emitted, microbial biomass (BIO) and humified organic matter (HUM). In Roth-C the ratio of HUM to BIO is set at 46:54. In turn, both these decomposition products (BIO and HUM) are converted to a mixture of CO2, BIO and HUM. The speed at which each of these types of organic matter are broken down are 10 for DPM, 0.3 for RPM, 0.66 for BIO and 0.02 for HUM.
This represents "k" in the formula above.

The ratio between CO2, BIO and HUM is determined by the soil clay content. A clay content of 35% is assumed, because approxi- mately 50% of the European top soil has a clay content between 28% and 98% (JRC, 2016), an average over entire Europe being 35%. The ratio is given by the following relationship (Krull et al., 2001):
CO2 (2) (BIO +

By combining this information on the initial carbon content of the soil and the quantity of carbon remaining in the soil after time (t) the fraction of carbon added to the soil that remains in the soil (sequestered) is determined per fertilizer product.

2.2. 2. 2. Background data and data quality assessment. Besides the data described above, some general data sources have been used. Emissions from diesel used by a tractor applying fer- tilizer products to land are taken into consideration according to Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Emissions to air and water from appli- cation of calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) during system expan- sion are based on Commissie Deskundigen Meststoffenwet (2013) and Quantis (2014).

The environmental impact of background processes is obtained from Ecoinvent v3.3 (Cut off) and Agri-footprint v3.0 (Economic allocation). The exact data used from the Ecoinvent and Agri- footprint databases can be found in the Supplementary data.

Data used for thermal conversion at BMC Moerdijk is primary data of good quality. Due to the annual data collection at BMC Moerdijk data could be crosschecked and is deemed highly reliable. For both composting and granulation data has been obtained from a treatment facility. The quality of these data is somewhat lower than for thermal conversion, because these treatment facilities collect data less often than BMC Moerdijk does. For both direct application of poultry litter and for co-digestion of poultry litter, no primary data was available, the data is based on estimations, so that data quality is lower.
2.2.3. Life cycle impact assessment
2.2.3.1. Software. The Life Cycle Impact Assessment is carried out in SimaPro 9.0.
2.2.3.2. Environmental impact categories. All midpoint impact cat- egories of the ReCiPe2016 methodology (v1.1, Hierarchist approach) are used (Huijbregts et al., 2017): global warming, stratospheric

ozone depletion, ionizing radiation, ozone formation (both impact on human health and terrestrial ecosystems), fine particulate matter formation, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophica- tion, marine eutrophication, terrestrial ecotoxicity, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human toxicity, (both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic), land use, mineral resource scarcity, fossil resource scarcity and water consumption.
45: A contribution analysis is carried out to determine which part of the functional unit has the largest environmental impact per envi- ronmental impact category. These impacts are explored in a sensi- tivity analysis. According to Igos et al. (2018), a sensitivity analysis is the minimum needed in assessing the uncertainty of LCA results.
46: Raw poultry litter contains nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and organic matter (see Table 1). As during thermal conversion of poultry litter practically all nitrogen is lost as N2 and all organic matter as CO2, poultry litter ash is not a N-fertilizer and the soil is not improved with organic matter, when poultry litter ash is applied. Losses of nitrogen and organic matter do not occur in the other treatment methods. Because phosphorus and potassium are not emitted to air during treatment of poultry litter by means of thermal conversion, composting, granulation or co-digestion, the same amount of these elements end up in the product in all treatment methods. As sources of phosphorus and potassium are finite, greater weight might be attached to the results for these two elements than to these for nitrogen and organic matter.

All five treatment methods produce different quantities and types of products that can serve as fertilizer. The number of fertil- izer equivalents of a given nutrient obtained per tonne of poultry litter is determined by multiplying the quantity of product by the concentration of the considered nutrient. This is further multiplied by the efficacy of the nutrient, i.e. the fraction of the nutrient that is available to a plant. The first-year efficacy is chosen, because, when farmers apply nutrients, they take into consideration the nutrient demand per year, and often overlook that applied fertilizers also have an efficacy after the first year.

van Schöll and Postma, 2016 compared the efficacy of the nu- trients in poultry litter ash and raw poultry litter to that in artificial fertilizers such as triple superphosphate (measured as P2O5), po- tassium sulfate (measured as K20), and calcium ammonium nitrate (measured as N). Van Schöll and Postma rely on information from Ehlert and Nelemans (2015b) for poultry litter ash and from CBAV (2016) for raw poultry litter. The first-year efficacy per fertilizer product in comparison to these artificial fertilizers can be found in the supplementary data under '2.1 Fertilizer equivalents'. For example, the efficacy of the nitrogen in raw poultry litter is 55% in comparison to the nitrogen in calcium ammonium nitrate. This means that, to make the same amount of nitrogen available to crops, approximately twice as much nitrogen in raw poultry needs to be applied to land than when calcium ammonium nitrate is used.

Besides the nutrients phosphate, potassium and nitrate, the fertilizer products also contain organic matter. For poultry litter ash, poultry litter compost, and poultry litter granulates, the organic matter content has been retrieved from the producers. For the digestate, the organic matter content has been calculated based on data from Reinhold (2005). From this organic matter content, the effective organic matter (EOM) present in the soil after one year is determined by means of the humification coefficients of the products determined by van Schöll and Postma, 2016. The humi- fication coefficient indicates the fraction of organic matter that, after one year, remains in the soil as humified organic matter.

van Schöll and Postma, 2016 compared the efficacy of the nu- trients in poultry litter ash and raw poultry litter to that in artificial fertilizers such as triple superphosphate (measured as P2O5), po- tassium sulfate (measured as K20), and calcium ammonium nitrate (measured as N). Van Schöll and Postma rely on information from Ehlert and Nelemans (2015b) for poultry litter ash and from CBAV (2016) for raw poultry litter. The first-year efficacy per fertilizer product in comparison to these artificial fertilizers can be found in the supplementary data under '2.1 Fertilizer equivalents'. For example, the efficacy of the nitrogen in raw poultry litter is 55% in comparison to the nitrogen in calcium ammonium nitrate. This means that, to make the same amount of nitrogen available to crops, approximately twice as much nitrogen in raw poultry needs to be applied to land than when calcium ammonium nitrate is used.

Besides the nutrients phosphate, potassium and nitrate, the fertilizer products also contain organic matter. For poultry litter ash, poultry litter compost, and poultry litter granulates, the organic matter content has been retrieved from the producers. For the digestate, the organic matter content has been calculated based on data from Reinhold (2005). From this organic matter content, the effective organic matter (EOM) present in the soil after one year is determined by means of the humification coefficients of the products determined by van Schöll and Postma, 2016. The humi- fication coefficient indicates the fraction of organic matter that, after one year, remains in the soil as humified organic matter.
50: Fig. 2 shows that per tonne of poultry litter treated the maximum quantity of fertilizer equivalents is 21 kg of triple su- perphosphate, 21 kg of potassium sulfate and 14.5 kg of nitrogen. This means that when applying the fertilizer product to land this is the quantity of artificial fertilizer that does not need to be applied to land, since the nutrient is available to the crop from the fertilizer product. The maximum effective organic matter produced (not shown in the figure) is 168 kg per tonne of litter treated (for granulation). Fig. 2 shows also that all treatment methods lead to the same amount of potassium (K20) fertilizer equivalent per tonne of poultry litter processed.

The end-products of all treatment methods (i.e. poultry litter ash, raw litter, compost, granulate and digestate) also contain the same amount of phosphorus (P2O5) per tonne of processed litter. The difference between the poultry litter ash and the other prod- ucts lies in the efficacy of the P2O5. For poultry litter ash the 1-year efficacy of the P2O5 ranges from 37% (Ehlert and Nelemans, 2015a) to 100% (Ehlert and Nelemans, 2015c), depending on the type of crop to which the poultry litter ash is applied. For the other prod- ucts, it is approximately 70%, but only an average could be found in literature (Ehlert, 2017). If the 1-year efficacy of P2O5 is less than 70% for poultry litter ash, less P2O5 becomes available within one year than with the other treatment methods. For thermal conver- sion of poultry litter the 1-year efficacy of phosphorus (as P2O5) may work out either better or worse, depending on the crop and soil type for which the poultry litter ash is used.

The end-products of all treatment methods contain different quantities of nitrogen equivalents. All nitrogen is lost in case of thermal conversion; during composting and granulation part of the nitrogen is lost. The largest quantity of nitrogen remains available when poultry litter is directly applied to land or when it is co- digested.
Subscript Fig. 2: Margin for Thermal conversion indicates the two literature values for the efficacy of poultry litter ash (37% is indicated by the blue bar and 100% by the top of the margin).

53: The results of the environmental impact assessment are given in Table 4. They are also graphically shown in Fig. 3, but normalized with respect to the treatment method with the highest impact in the respective impact category. Three groups of impact categories (see Fig. 3) can be distinguished: in the first group (starting from
Fig. 2. Fertilizer equivalents delivered from the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter as available in the first year to plants.

left in Fig. 3) granulation has the highest impact and thermal conversion the lowest, in the second one direct land application has the highest impact and thermal conversion the lowest, and in the third one granulation has the highest impact and co-digestion the lowest.
Subscript Fig. 3: Margin for Thermal conversion indicates the two literature values for the efficacy of poultry litter ash (37% and 100%).

In the first group of impact categories thermal conversion of poultry litter is the most environmentally friendly method, number 2 is co-digestion of poultry litter; this is followed by direct appli- cation and composting, which have a very similar impact and finally by granulation. There is a striking correlation between the impact and the production of electricity by the treatment methods: thermal conversion and co-digestion produce electricity, 25% more electricity being produced by thermal conversion than by co- digestion; direct application, composting and granulation do not produce electricity, but, on the contrary, electricity from the Dutch national grid must be used to fulfil the functional unit and in the process. The difference in environmental impact between the latter three methods can partly be explained by difference in energy use during the processing of the litter: more energy is used for gran- ulation than for direct application and composting. Electricity production also has an impact on the results in the other impact categories but is most dominant in the impact categories in the first group.

The error margin in Fig. 3 shows the difference between a phosphate first year efficacy of 37% and 100% in the case of thermal conversion, depending on the crop for which the poultry litter ash is applied. The basis results correspond to 100% efficacy and the error bar to 37% efficacy. Since the efficacy of the phosphate in the digestate of co-digestion is 70%, phosphate is added in the system expansion. If the efficacy of the poultry litter ash is 37%, phosphate is added in the system expansion for thermal conversion. The impact of supplementing phosphate fertilizer is especially pro- nounced in the impact categories 'metal depletion' and 'terrestrial ecotoxicity', because phosphate rock is depleted (metal depletion) and emissions of phosphorus occur during the production of fertilizer.

The second group consists of the impact categories 'strato- spheric ozone depletion', 'fine particulate matter formation' and 'terrestrial acidification'. The environmental impact in this group is mainly determined by emissions during application of the fertilizer products (direct application and co-digestion) and emissions dur- ing treatment (composting). The most environmentally friendly method in this group is thermal conversion. Direct application and

co-digestion are the least environmentally friendly in these impact categories, because of the high emissions of mainly ammonia and nitrate during application of the fertilizer products. Emissions also occur during application in the other treatment routes, but emis- sions per kg of nutrient differ between the different types of fer- tilizer products. For example, the application of artificial fertilizer leads to fewer emissions per kg of nutrient than the application of raw manure directly to land.

The last group of impact categories consists of 'ozone formation' both on human health and on terrestrial ecosystems. Ozone for- mation is due to NO emitted from the incineration plant, from diesel combustion in agricultural machinery used for application of the fertilizer products to land, or from the production of electricity supplemented in the system expansion.

The results in Fig. 3 show that thermal conversion of poultry litter has the lowest impact for all environmental impact categories, except for photochemical oxidation, where co-digestion prevails. For all other impact categories co-digestion has the second lowest impact, except for particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification. Granulation of poultry litter appears the least envi- ronmentally friendly for all but two impact categories, because a large amount of energy is used for drying of the poultry litter. Direct application and composting of poultry litter have a comparable environmental impact for most impact categories and have an environmental impact between thermal conversion and granulation.

The division of the impact per impact category per treatment method over the different parts of the studied system leads to several interesting observations. Firstly, carbon sequestration con- tributes between 13 and 25% to the climate change impact of the different treatment routes where carbon is added to the soil. Sec- ondly, the addition of nitrogen can contribute as much as 100% to the results on several environmental impact categories. High con- tributions (>70%) can be found in the impact categories 'fossil resource scarcity', 'global warming', 'marine eutrophication' and 'stratospheric ozone depletion' for thermal conversion due to a necessity to add additional nitrogen fertilizer and phosphate Thirdly, the addition of phosphate can contribute as much as 92% to the results on 'mineral resource scarcity'. High contributions (>70%) can be found in the impact category 'mineral resource scarcity' for all treatment routes other than thermal conversion due to a necessity to add additional phosphate fertilizer. Lastly, the addition of electricity production has a high contribution (>70%) in all the impact categories shown in Group 1 in Fig. 3 for the treat- ment routes composting and granulation, direct application, and composting. In some instances, this is also the case for co-digestion.

Lastly, the addition of electricity production has a high contribution (>70%) in all the impact categories shown in Group 1 in Fig. 3 for the treat- ment routes composting and granulation, direct application, and composting. In some instances, this is also the case for co-digestion.
58: The results of the environmental impact assessment are given in Table 4. They are also graphically shown in Fig. 3, but normalized with respect to the treatment method with the highest impact in the respective impact category. Three groups of impact categories (see Fig. 3) can be distinguished: in the first group (starting from
Fig. 2. Fertilizer equivalents delivered from the treatment of 1 tonne of poultry litter as available in the first year to plants.

left in Fig. 3) granulation has the highest impact and thermal conversion the lowest, in the second one direct land application has the highest impact and thermal conversion the lowest, and in the third one granulation has the highest impact and co-digestion the lowest.
Subscript Fig. 3: Margin for Thermal conversion indicates the two literature values for the efficacy of poultry litter ash (37% and 100%).

In the first group of impact categories thermal conversion of poultry litter is the most environmentally friendly method, number 2 is co-digestion of poultry litter; this is followed by direct appli- cation and composting, which have a very similar impact and finally by granulation. There is a striking correlation between the impact and the production of electricity by the treatment methods: thermal conversion and co-digestion produce electricity, 25% more electricity being produced by thermal conversion than by co- digestion; direct application, composting and granulation do not produce electricity, but, on the contrary, electricity from the Dutch national grid must be used to fulfil the functional unit and in the process. The difference in environmental impact between the latter three methods can partly be explained by difference in energy use during the processing of the litter: more energy is used for gran- ulation than for direct application and composting. Electricity production also has an impact on the results in the other impact categories but is most dominant in the impact categories in the first group.

The error margin in Fig. 3 shows the difference between a phosphate first year efficacy of 37% and 100% in the case of thermal conversion, depending on the crop for which the poultry litter ash is applied. The basis results correspond to 100% efficacy and the error bar to 37% efficacy. Since the efficacy of the phosphate in the digestate of co-digestion is 70%, phosphate is added in the system expansion. If the efficacy of the poultry litter ash is 37%, phosphate is added in the system expansion for thermal conversion. The impact of supplementing phosphate fertilizer is especially pro- nounced in the impact categories 'metal depletion' and 'terrestrial ecotoxicity', because phosphate rock is depleted (metal depletion) and emissions of phosphorus occur during the production of fertilizer.

The second group consists of the impact categories 'strato- spheric ozone depletion', 'fine particulate matter formation' and 'terrestrial acidification'. The environmental impact in this group is mainly determined by emissions during application of the fertilizer products (direct application and co-digestion) and emissions dur- ing treatment (composting). The most environmentally friendly method in this group is thermal conversion. Direct application and

co-digestion are the least environmentally friendly in these impact categories, because of the high emissions of mainly ammonia and nitrate during application of the fertilizer products. Emissions also occur during application in the other treatment routes, but emis- sions per kg of nutrient differ between the different types of fer- tilizer products. For example, the application of artificial fertilizer leads to fewer emissions per kg of nutrient than the application of raw manure directly to land.

The last group of impact categories consists of 'ozone formation' both on human health and on terrestrial ecosystems. Ozone for- mation is due to NO emitted from the incineration plant, from diesel combustion in agricultural machinery used for application of the fertilizer products to land, or from the production of electricity supplemented in the system expansion.

The results in Fig. 3 show that thermal conversion of poultry litter has the lowest impact for all environmental impact categories, except for photochemical oxidation, where co-digestion prevails. For all other impact categories co-digestion has the second lowest impact, except for particulate matter formation and terrestrial acidification. Granulation of poultry litter appears the least envi- ronmentally friendly for all but two impact categories, because a large amount of energy is used for drying of the poultry litter. Direct application and composting of poultry litter have a comparable environmental impact for most impact categories and have an environmental impact between thermal conversion and granulation.

The division of the impact per impact category per treatment method over the different parts of the studied system leads to several interesting observations. Firstly, carbon sequestration con- tributes between 13 and 25% to the climate change impact of the different treatment routes where carbon is added to the soil. Sec- ondly, the addition of nitrogen can contribute as much as 100% to the results on several environmental impact categories. High con- tributions (>70%) can be found in the impact categories 'fossil resource scarcity', 'global warming', 'marine eutrophication' and 'stratospheric ozone depletion' for thermal conversion due to a necessity to add additional nitrogen fertilizer and phosphate Thirdly, the addition of phosphate can contribute as much as 92% to the results on 'mineral resource scarcity'. High contributions (>70%) can be found in the impact category 'mineral resource scarcity' for all treatment routes other than thermal conversion due to a necessity to add additional phosphate fertilizer. Lastly, the addition of electricity production has a high contribution (>70%) in all the impact categories shown in Group 1 in Fig. 3 for the treat- ment routes composting and granulation, direct application, and composting. In some instances, this is also the case for co-digestion.

62: In this section normalization and sensitivity analysis will be discussed and the results will be compared with other studies.
63: Normalization based on the normalization set available in SimaPro in ReCiPe2016 Endpoint World H/A was applied to get a better insight into the relative importance of the different impact categories. The environmental impact of the functional unit was compared with the environmental impact per year of one World citizen. The environmental impact per citizen as described by Sleeswijk et al., 2008 was determined by dividing the total envi- ronmental impact on each impact category by the amount of in- habitants in the World in 2000. Normalization shows (Fig. 4) that for only two of the impact categories treatment of one tonne of poultry litter corresponds to more than 1% of the normalized environmental impact per year of one World citizen. These important impact categories are: 'climate change' and 'fine partic- ulate matter formation'.

Normalization is not the only method to compare the impact categories. Alternatives could be to study the impact based on endpoint modeling, as available in ReCiPe2016, or to monetize the impacts based on damage costs. The latter is unfortunately not available to be applied to ReCiPe2016, but could be applied to ReCiPe2008 based on the Environmental Prices Handbook devel- oped by CE Delft (2018). From endpoint modeling in ReCiPe2016, it is apparent that the environmental impact categories 'Global warming' and 'Fine particulate matter formation' together account for at least 85% of the impact on the endpoint "Human Health" for all treatment methods. 'Global warming' and Terrestrial acidifica- tion' together account for at least 80% of the impact on the endpoint "Ecosystems" for all treatment methods, and 'Fossil resource scar- city' accounts for 99% of the impact on the endpoint "Resources" for all treatment methods.
For each of these main impact categories, thermal conversion is the most environmentally friendly treatment method.

65: It is to be expected that due to a shift to renewable energy in the future the electricity mix will become less reliant on fossil re- sources than at this moment. The positive results of thermal con- version and co-digestion in Group 1 (See Fig. 3) are largely explained by the fact that they produce renewable, carbon-neutral electricity with relatively little emissions compared to e.g. coal combustion. In case of a shift of the current Dutch electricity mix (80% coal and natural gas based, CBS, 2019c) to more electricity from solar and wind, this advantage may reduce significantly.

Instead of using the biogas from anaerobic digestion, typically having 55% CH4 content, in a CHP, as is currently often done in Germany, it can also be upgraded to produce green gas/bio- methane, which has a CH4 content of 95-98%, and is equivalent to natural gas except for its origin. Green gas can e.g. be used as transportation fuel (CNG) instead of diesel. The sensitivity of the results to this change was assessed. The fact that no electricity is produced needs to be compensated for, but the green gas replaces both diesel production and emissions during combustion (con- sumption of the diesel). This change would make co-digestion the most environmentally friendly option in four impact categories where thermal conversion was the most environmentally friendly before: 'climate change', 'fossil depletion', 'natural land trans- formation' and 'ozone depletion', but the difference between the two routes is not very large. However, the change would also in- crease the environmental impact in five environmental impact categories: 'agricultural land occupation', 'freshwater ecotoxicity', 'freshwater eutrophication', 'marine ecotoxicity' and 'human toxicity'. Since 'climate change' and 'fossil depletion' are the most important impact categories according to the normalization, it can be concluded that with production of green gas 'co-digestion' together with thermal conversion are the most interesting treat- ment methods.

Besides electricity, a CHP in general also produces heat. As it is unclear whether this heat is always used and whether it would avoid the production of heat from e.g. natural gas, it has not been modelled in the analysis. As the results may be sensitive to this choice, the sensitivity of the results to complete marketing of the heat produced in the CHP from biogas was analyzed. It was
Fig. 4. Environmental impact of treatment of one tonne of poultry litter normalized with the ReCiPe2016 H/A methodology.

assumed that 36% of the LHV of biogas is transformed into heat (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). If it is assumed that the produced heat is used in the process instead of heat that would otherwise be produced with natural gas, co-digestion is the most environmen- tally friendly of the five treatment methods when it comes to 'fossil depletion' and 'climate change', but the difference with thermal conversion is not very large. In addition, the use of this heat would lead to a (minor) reduction of the environmental impact of co- digestion on nine other impact categories. This means that the environmental benefit of co-digestion comes closer to that of thermal conversion, but thermal conversion still has a lower environmental impact than co-digestion on most impact cate- gories. It can again be concluded that also in this case both 'thermal conversion' and 'co-digestion' are the most interesting treatment methods.

The effects of storage of poultry litter were not included in the basic analysis, as they are not attributes of the technologies studied, but are due to local circumstances. For all treatment methods storage occurs to a certain extent. Storage time is expected to be the longest for direct application of poultry litter to land and shorter in the case of the other treatment methods. During storage N20, CH4 and NH3 are emitted. These have an impact on different impact categories of which only climate change falls within group 1 (the group in which thermal conversion has the lowest environmental impact). When an emission of approximately 4.5 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of litter stored per month (estimated based on Hoek and Schijndel, 2006; Velthof et al., 2012; IPCC, 2006) and an approxi- mate difference in climate change impact between thermal con- version and direct application of 300 kg CO2 eq. and between co- digestion and direct application of 225 kg CO2 eq. are taken into account, 'thermal conversion' and co-digestion would no longer be the most environmentally friendly routes if the difference of stor- age time is 66 and 51 months in comparison to direct application of poultry litter, respectively. Such a long storage time is highly unlikely.

The different produced fertilizer products, e.g. poultry litter ash, and poultry litter granulated, can be applied in different places. The impact of transportation was not included in the basic analysis, as it is not an attribute of the technologies studied but related to local legislation and market circumstances. As some fertilizer products such as poultry litter ash are easier to transport than others and the quantity of fertilizer product produced per treatment route differs, including the environmental impact of transporting the products will have a different influence on the results per treatment route. When the same argumentation is applied as for storage of poultry litter, the difference in transport distance between raw poultry litter and poultry litter ash needs to exceed 713 km (in the case of terrestrial ecotoxicity) and 3400 km by truck in the case of the other impact categories, for thermal conversion to no longer be more environmentally friendly than direct application in the impact categories in group 1 (the group in which thermal conversion has the lowest environmental impact). For climate change and fossil depletion, this is more than 10,000 km, 4000 km being the distance between the Netherlands and Kazan in Russia. Such a long difference in transport is unlikely to occur. The difference in transportation distance between raw poultry manure and poultry litter digestate needs to exceed 54 km (in the case of terrestrial ecotoxicity) and 490 km for the other impact categories in group 1, for co-digestion to be no longer more environmentally friendly than direct application. For climate change and fossil depletion this is more than 1300 km, 500 km being the distance between Amsterdam and Paris. Such a differ- ence in transport distance could in theory occur, but is quite un- likely for the transport of digestate.

In the base case analysis, a choice has been to characterize the nitrogen fertilizer as a CAN fertilizer and the phosphate fertilizer as a triple superphosphate fertilizer. However, triple superphosphate is not the most common phosphate fertilizer applied in the Netherlands, but it is the only fertilizer that is not a combination fertilizer of e.g. nitrogen and phosphate; the most common is

ammonium phosphate (30%) (International Fertilizer Industry Association, 2019). Considering a different P-fertilizer would not change the results significantly, since mineral resource extraction and terrestrial ecotoxicity are the only impact categories where the phosphate fertilizer is the most important factor (more important than electricity supply). Since triple superphosphate has a 20% higher impact on mineral resource scarcity and a 5% lower impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity than ammonium phosphate, choosing ammonium phosphate over triple superphosphate would not change the results significantly in these impact categories either. Finally, because of lack of data on the heavy metal content in the different types of fertilizers this topic was not included in the analysis. If it would be assumed that all heavy metals end up in the final fertilizer product, the ratio of heavy metals to the nutrient content of phosphate and nitrogen would differ per treatment method. In that case the results about thermal conversion might possibly change, if the efficacy of the phosphate is 37%. Since the poultry litter ash cannot be used as nitrogen fertilizer, this would not have a significant impact on the results.

Lastly in the base case analysis, the choice has been made to not include other options for increasing or maintaining carbon content in the soil, such as the use of peat and straw. The carbon in the different fertilizer products could be seen as an additional product delivered as part of the functional unit (shown in Table 2). This was not considered since overall soil organic carbon content in the EU has been declining (Panagos et al., 2013) and it can thus not be seen as common praxis to maintain soil carbon content. Not considering other methods available to increase soil carbon content can, how- ever, be seen as a limitation to this study.

The positive results of thermal con- version and co-digestion in Group 1 (See Fig. 3) are largely explained by the fact that they produce renewable, carbon-neutral electricity with relatively little emissions compared to e.g. coal combustion. In case of a shift of the current Dutch electricity mix (80% coal and natural gas based, CBS, 2019c) to more electricity from solar and wind, this advantage may reduce significantly.

Instead of using the biogas from anaerobic digestion, typically having 55% CH4 content, in a CHP, as is currently often done in Germany, it can also be upgraded to produce green gas/bio- methane, which has a CH4 content of 95-98%, and is equivalent to natural gas except for its origin. Green gas can e.g. be used as transportation fuel (CNG) instead of diesel. The sensitivity of the results to this change was assessed. The fact that no electricity is produced needs to be compensated for, but the green gas replaces both diesel production and emissions during combustion (con- sumption of the diesel). This change would make co-digestion the most environmentally friendly option in four impact categories where thermal conversion was the most environmentally friendly before: 'climate change', 'fossil depletion', 'natural land trans- formation' and 'ozone depletion', but the difference between the two routes is not very large. However, the change would also in- crease the environmental impact in five environmental impact categories: 'agricultural land occupation', 'freshwater ecotoxicity', 'freshwater eutrophication', 'marine ecotoxicity' and 'human toxicity'. Since 'climate change' and 'fossil depletion' are the most important impact categories according to the normalization, it can be concluded that with production of green gas 'co-digestion' together with thermal conversion are the most interesting treat- ment methods.

Besides electricity, a CHP in general also produces heat. As it is unclear whether this heat is always used and whether it would avoid the production of heat from e.g. natural gas, it has not been modelled in the analysis. As the results may be sensitive to this choice, the sensitivity of the results to complete marketing of the heat produced in the CHP from biogas was analyzed. It was
Fig. 4. Environmental impact of treatment of one tonne of poultry litter normalized with the ReCiPe2016 H/A methodology.

assumed that 36% of the LHV of biogas is transformed into heat (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). If it is assumed that the produced heat is used in the process instead of heat that would otherwise be produced with natural gas, co-digestion is the most environmen- tally friendly of the five treatment methods when it comes to 'fossil depletion' and 'climate change', but the difference with thermal conversion is not very large. In addition, the use of this heat would lead to a (minor) reduction of the environmental impact of co- digestion on nine other impact categories. This means that the environmental benefit of co-digestion comes closer to that of thermal conversion, but thermal conversion still has a lower environmental impact than co-digestion on most impact cate- gories. It can again be concluded that also in this case both 'thermal conversion' and 'co-digestion' are the most interesting treatment methods.

The effects of storage of poultry litter were not included in the basic analysis, as they are not attributes of the technologies studied, but are due to local circumstances. For all treatment methods storage occurs to a certain extent. Storage time is expected to be the longest for direct application of poultry litter to land and shorter in the case of the other treatment methods. During storage N20, CH4 and NH3 are emitted. These have an impact on different impact categories of which only climate change falls within group 1 (the group in which thermal conversion has the lowest environmental impact). When an emission of approximately 4.5 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of litter stored per month (estimated based on Hoek and Schijndel, 2006; Velthof et al., 2012; IPCC, 2006) and an approxi- mate difference in climate change impact between thermal con- version and direct application of 300 kg CO2 eq. and between co- digestion and direct application of 225 kg CO2 eq. are taken into account, 'thermal conversion' and co-digestion would no longer be the most environmentally friendly routes if the difference of stor- age time is 66 and 51 months in comparison to direct application of poultry litter, respectively. Such a long storage time is highly unlikely.

The different produced fertilizer products, e.g. poultry litter ash, and poultry litter granulated, can be applied in different places. The impact of transportation was not included in the basic analysis, as it is not an attribute of the technologies studied but related to local legislation and market circumstances. As some fertilizer products such as poultry litter ash are easier to transport than others and the quantity of fertilizer product produced per treatment route differs, including the environmental impact of transporting the products will have a different influence on the results per treatment route. When the same argumentation is applied as for storage of poultry litter, the difference in transport distance between raw poultry litter and poultry litter ash needs to exceed 713 km (in the case of terrestrial ecotoxicity) and 3400 km by truck in the case of the other impact categories, for thermal conversion to no longer be more environmentally friendly than direct application in the impact categories in group 1 (the group in which thermal conversion has the lowest environmental impact). For climate change and fossil depletion, this is more than 10,000 km, 4000 km being the distance between the Netherlands and Kazan in Russia. Such a long difference in transport is unlikely to occur. The difference in transportation distance between raw poultry manure and poultry litter digestate needs to exceed 54 km (in the case of terrestrial ecotoxicity) and 490 km for the other impact categories in group 1, for co-digestion to be no longer more environmentally friendly than direct application. For climate change and fossil depletion this is more than 1300 km, 500 km being the distance between Amsterdam and Paris. Such a differ- ence in transport distance could in theory occur, but is quite un- likely for the transport of digestate.

In the base case analysis, a choice has been to characterize the nitrogen fertilizer as a CAN fertilizer and the phosphate fertilizer as a triple superphosphate fertilizer. However, triple superphosphate is not the most common phosphate fertilizer applied in the Netherlands, but it is the only fertilizer that is not a combination fertilizer of e.g. nitrogen and phosphate; the most common is

ammonium phosphate (30%) (International Fertilizer Industry Association, 2019). Considering a different P-fertilizer would not change the results significantly, since mineral resource extraction and terrestrial ecotoxicity are the only impact categories where the phosphate fertilizer is the most important factor (more important than electricity supply). Since triple superphosphate has a 20% higher impact on mineral resource scarcity and a 5% lower impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity than ammonium phosphate, choosing ammonium phosphate over triple superphosphate would not change the results significantly in these impact categories either. Finally, because of lack of data on the heavy metal content in the different types of fertilizers this topic was not included in the analysis. If it would be assumed that all heavy metals end up in the final fertilizer product, the ratio of heavy metals to the nutrient content of phosphate and nitrogen would differ per treatment method. In that case the results about thermal conversion might possibly change, if the efficacy of the phosphate is 37%. Since the poultry litter ash cannot be used as nitrogen fertilizer, this would not have a significant impact on the results.

Lastly in the base case analysis, the choice has been made to not include other options for increasing or maintaining carbon content in the soil, such as the use of peat and straw. The carbon in the different fertilizer products could be seen as an additional product delivered as part of the functional unit (shown in Table 2). This was not considered since overall soil organic carbon content in the EU has been declining (Panagos et al., 2013) and it can thus not be seen as common praxis to maintain soil carbon content. Not considering other methods available to increase soil carbon content can, how- ever, be seen as a limitation to this study.
