The effects of storage of poultry litter were not included in the basic analysis, as they are not attributes of the technologies studied, but are due to local circumstances. For all treatment methods storage occurs to a certain extent. Storage time is expected to be the longest for direct application of poultry litter to land and shorter in the case of the other treatment methods. During storage N20, CH4 and NH3 are emitted. These have an impact on different impact categories of which only climate change falls within group 1 (the group in which thermal conversion has the lowest environmental impact). When an emission of approximately 4.5 kg CO2 eq. per tonne of litter stored per month (estimated based on Hoek and Schijndel, 2006; Velthof et al., 2012; IPCC, 2006) and an approxi- mate difference in climate change impact between thermal con- version and direct application of 300 kg CO2 eq. and between co- digestion and direct application of 225 kg CO2 eq. are taken into account, 'thermal conversion' and co-digestion would no longer be the most environmentally friendly routes if the difference of stor- age time is 66 and 51 months in comparison to direct application of poultry litter, respectively. Such a long storage time is highly unlikely.

The different produced fertilizer products, e.g. poultry litter ash, and poultry litter granulated, can be applied in different places. The impact of transportation was not included in the basic analysis, as it is not an attribute of the technologies studied but related to local legislation and market circumstances. As some fertilizer products such as poultry litter ash are easier to transport than others and the quantity of fertilizer product produced per treatment route differs, including the environmental impact of transporting the products will have a different influence on the results per treatment route. When the same argumentation is applied as for storage of poultry litter, the difference in transport distance between raw poultry litter and poultry litter ash needs to exceed 713 km (in the case of terrestrial ecotoxicity) and 3400 km by truck in the case of the other impact categories, for thermal conversion to no longer be more environmentally friendly than direct application in the impact categories in group 1 (the group in which thermal conversion has the lowest environmental impact). For climate change and fossil depletion, this is more than 10,000 km, 4000 km being the distance between the Netherlands and Kazan in Russia. Such a long difference in transport is unlikely to occur. The difference in transportation distance between raw poultry manure and poultry litter digestate needs to exceed 54 km (in the case of terrestrial ecotoxicity) and 490 km for the other impact categories in group 1, for co-digestion to be no longer more environmentally friendly than direct application. For climate change and fossil depletion this is more than 1300 km, 500 km being the distance between Amsterdam and Paris. Such a differ- ence in transport distance could in theory occur, but is quite un- likely for the transport of digestate.

In the base case analysis, a choice has been to characterize the nitrogen fertilizer as a CAN fertilizer and the phosphate fertilizer as a triple superphosphate fertilizer. However, triple superphosphate is not the most common phosphate fertilizer applied in the Netherlands, but it is the only fertilizer that is not a combination fertilizer of e.g. nitrogen and phosphate; the most common is

ammonium phosphate (30%) (International Fertilizer Industry Association, 2019). Considering a different P-fertilizer would not change the results significantly, since mineral resource extraction and terrestrial ecotoxicity are the only impact categories where the phosphate fertilizer is the most important factor (more important than electricity supply). Since triple superphosphate has a 20% higher impact on mineral resource scarcity and a 5% lower impact on terrestrial ecotoxicity than ammonium phosphate, choosing ammonium phosphate over triple superphosphate would not change the results significantly in these impact categories either. Finally, because of lack of data on the heavy metal content in the different types of fertilizers this topic was not included in the analysis. If it would be assumed that all heavy metals end up in the final fertilizer product, the ratio of heavy metals to the nutrient content of phosphate and nitrogen would differ per treatment method. In that case the results about thermal conversion might possibly change, if the efficacy of the phosphate is 37%. Since the poultry litter ash cannot be used as nitrogen fertilizer, this would not have a significant impact on the results.

Lastly in the base case analysis, the choice has been made to not include other options for increasing or maintaining carbon content in the soil, such as the use of peat and straw. The carbon in the different fertilizer products could be seen as an additional product delivered as part of the functional unit (shown in Table 2). This was not considered since overall soil organic carbon content in the EU has been declining (Panagos et al., 2013) and it can thus not be seen as common praxis to maintain soil carbon content. Not considering other methods available to increase soil carbon content can, how- ever, be seen as a limitation to this study.
72: It is to be expected that due to a shift to renewable energy in the future the electricity mix will become less reliant on fossil re- sources than at this moment. The positive results of thermal con- version and co-digestion in Group 1 (See Fig. 3) are largely explained by the fact that they produce renewable, carbon-neutral electricity with relatively little emissions compared to e.g. coal combustion. In case of a shift of the current Dutch electricity mix (80% coal and natural gas based, CBS, 2019c) to more electricity from solar and wind, this advantage may reduce significantly.

Instead of using the biogas from anaerobic digestion, typically having 55% CH4 content, in a CHP, as is currently often done in Germany, it can also be upgraded to produce green gas/bio- methane, which has a CH4 content of 95-98%, and is equivalent to natural gas except for its origin. Green gas can e.g. be used as transportation fuel (CNG) instead of diesel. The sensitivity of the results to this change was assessed. The fact that no electricity is produced needs to be compensated for, but the green gas replaces both diesel production and emissions during combustion (con- sumption of the diesel). This change would make co-digestion the most environmentally friendly option in four impact categories where thermal conversion was the most environmentally friendly before: 'climate change', 'fossil depletion', 'natural land trans- formation' and 'ozone depletion', but the difference between the two routes is not very large. However, the change would also in- crease the environmental impact in five environmental impact categories: 'agricultural land occupation', 'freshwater ecotoxicity', 'freshwater eutrophication', 'marine ecotoxicity' and 'human toxicity'. Since 'climate change' and 'fossil depletion' are the most important impact categories according to the normalization, it can be concluded that with production of green gas 'co-digestion' together with thermal conversion are the most interesting treat- ment methods.

Besides electricity, a CHP in general also produces heat. As it is unclear whether this heat is always used and whether it would avoid the production of heat from e.g. natural gas, it has not been modelled in the analysis. As the results may be sensitive to this choice, the sensitivity of the results to complete marketing of the heat produced in the CHP from biogas was analyzed. It was
Fig. 4. Environmental impact of treatment of one tonne of poultry litter normalized with the ReCiPe2016 H/A methodology.

assumed that 36% of the LHV of biogas is transformed into heat (Velghe and Wierinck, 2013). If it is assumed that the produced heat is used in the process instead of heat that would otherwise be produced with natural gas, co-digestion is the most environmen- tally friendly of the five treatment methods when it comes to 'fossil depletion' and 'climate change', but the difference with thermal conversion is not very large. In addition, the use of this heat would lead to a (minor) reduction of the environmental impact of co- digestion on nine other impact categories. This means that the environmental benefit of co-digestion comes closer to that of thermal conversion, but thermal conversion still has a lower environmental impact than co-digestion on most impact cate- gories. 74: <header>4.3. Comparison of results with other studies </header>

Billen et al. (2015) compared the environmental impact of thermal conversion of poultry litter and of direct land application of poultry litter. Although some relevant environmental impact cat- legories e.g. 'natural land transformation' and 'freshwater eutro- phication' were not considered in this study and a different methodological approach was taken, both studies arrive at the same overall conclusion: in the Netherlands thermal conversion of poultry litter is more environmentally friendly than direct land application of poultry litter.

Williams et al. (2016) made a similar comparison as Billen et al. (2015), but for turkey litter, and concluded that thermal conversion has an environmental benefit in comparison to direct land appli- cation. Williams et al. (2016) expected similar results for broiler litter.

No other studies considering the environmental impact of the treatment of poultry litter have been conducted, but studies of the energy potential of treatment of poultry litter have been conducted. For example, Tanczuk et al. (2019) compared the energy potential of four different treatment methods for poultry litter, and concluded that thermal conversion by means of a fluidized bed incinerator has a higher energy potential than digestion of poultry litter. This is in line with the conclusions drawn in the present study.
75: <header>4.3. Comparison of results with other studies </header>

No other studies considering the environmental impact of the treatment of poultry litter have been conducted, but studies of the energy potential of treatment of poultry litter have been conducted. For example, Tanczuk et al. (2019) compared the energy potential of four different treatment methods for poultry litter, and concluded that thermal conversion by means of a fluidized bed incinerator has a higher energy potential than digestion of poultry litter. This is in line with the conclusions drawn in the present study.
76: The aim of this study was to compare the environmental per- formance of five different treatment methods for poultry litter: direct land application, composting, composting and granulation, co-digestion, and thermal conversion. These five methods are to date all applied to Dutch poultry litter and data were obtained relative to the relevant installations.
It was concluded from the attributional LCA that thermal con- version is currently preferable from an environmental perspective
to the four other treatment methods. Thermal conversion e.g. efficiently reduces greenhouse gas emissions and ammonia emissions.

The lower environmental impact of thermal conversion can in the Dutch situation for a large part be attributed to the fact that thermal conversion produces a large quantity of renewable, carbon-neutral electricity per tonne of poultry litter contrary to direct land application, composting and granulation. This is because the Dutch electricity mix largely (80% in 2017) consists of electricity generated from fossil fuels), and electricity production has a high environmental impact in various impact categories. Also, co-digestion of poultry litter produces electricity, but less than thermal conversion. The conclusions are therefore valid for the Netherlands and remain valid for other geographical areas with a surplus of poultry litter and a high carbon footprint of electricity production. To determine which treatment method is most ad- vantageous in other countries, the specific local conditions including the electricity mix will need to be taken into consideration.

It was also shown that it is important to take the nutrient balance into account for nitrogen and phosphate fertilizers and that the effective organic matter and associated carbon seques- tration play a role in the difference between the different treat- ment routes.

In this study treatment of average poultry litter not containing excessive amounts of non-natural, toxic chemicals, was considered. In some instances poultry litter may contain organic pollutants, an example being the recent contamination with fipronil. In such a case, hygienization becomes more important than under normal circumstances. An additional benefit of thermal conversion in comparison to the other treatment methods is then that toxic compounds are destroyed and do not end up on agricultural fields. When fipronil was found in poultry litter, the only treatment method allowed in Belgium, Germany, and the Netherlands was thermal conversion. These abnormal circumstances are not considered in the chosen functional unit and are not included in the LCA scores, but they are relevant nonetheless.
